title: Recapping the accelerating decline in ‘Minimum Demand’ levels across the NEM, at the end of 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping the accelerating decline in ‘Minimum Demand’ levels across the NEM, at the end of 2023," posted at 31-12-2023 04:49:00 PM is: This article provides an update on the record low levels of electricity demand across various regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia for the year 2023. Significant demand decreases have been observed in states like Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and mentions historical context from Tasmania as well. These reductions in demand are indicative of a broader shift influenced by various factors, potentially including energy efficiency measures and increased renewable energy penetration, such as rooftop solar PV systems. As such, these events could have consequences for electricity market dynamics, including pricing and grid stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing as of 2023. Its root_cause is: Increasing penetration of renewable energy and energy efficiency measures. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 1st October 2023, 29th October 2023, 31st December 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide (including QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Energy market trend. Its causes are: Increased renewable energy generation, energy efficiency measures, and changes in consumer behavior energy usage.

title: Recapping lowest points for demand today in Victoria (Part 4)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping lowest points for demand today in Victoria (Part 4)" posted at 31-12-2023 04:14:00 PM is: The article discusses the unusually low electricity demand observed in Victoria on December 31, 2023. The lowest recorded point for 'Market Demand' was 1,456MW at the 13:00 dispatch interval, while 'Operational Demand' reached a minimum of 1,564MW by the half-hour ending at 13:00. This marks a significant reduction from the prior set record on November 12, 2023. AEMO issued Market Notice 112942 to cancel the Minimum System Load 1 condition for the VIC Region on this date, citing reasons detailed in previous notices; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Seasonal and operational factors leading to reduced demand. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 31-12-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Lower than anticipated electricity demand in the region leading to the cancellation of MSL1 condition by AEMO.

title: Also new ‘lowest ever’ point for Market Demand in South Australia on Sunday 31st December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Also new ‘lowest ever’ point for Market Demand in South Australia on Sunday 31st December 2023" posted at 31-12-2023 01:41:00 PM is: The article reports a record low in market demand for electricity in South Australia, with demand reaching –45MW at 12:30 NEM time on December 31st, 2023, surpassing the previous record low of –37.14MW on October 1st, 2023. This situation illustrates a continued decline in electricity demand in the region. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Unusually high levels of local electricity generation exceeding demand, likely due to high solar generation and low consumption. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-12-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand and supply mismatch. Its causes are: Record low demand, high local electricity generation, historical reference to previous low on 1st October 2023.

title: Part 3 … Victorian demand holding up at higher levels than successive AEMO forecasts
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Part 3 … Victorian demand holding up at higher levels than successive AEMO forecasts" posted at 31-12-2023 01:21:00 PM is: The article discusses the discrepancy between actual market demand in Victoria and the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) forecasts. Despite AEMO's predictions of a lower demand, actual demand has remained higher, highlighting a conflict in forecast models and emphasizing that models are not always reflective of reality. This ongoing situation invites further investigation to understand the underlying causes of this disparity.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Forecast model discrepancies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 31-12-2023 01:21:00 PM. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Model forecast error. Its causes are: Incorrect predictions from AEMO's forecast models.

title: Part 2 – What’s running in Victoria today (apart from strong rooftop PV)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Part 2 – What’s running in Victoria today (apart from strong rooftop PV)?" posted at 31-12-2023 12:47:00 PM is: The article discusses the exceptionally low market demand for electricity in Victoria on December 31, 2023, which has reached levels below previous records. Electricity supply is primarily from rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems and brown coal units operating near their minimum load. Prices in both Victoria and South Australia are low, dissuading additional generation sources from operating. Although the grid currently has sufficient inertia to remain stable, the high output from rooftop PV systems poses the risk of coal units going offline if demand drops further, potentially destabilizing the grid's operational state. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing. Its root cause is: Excessive rooftop PV generation reducing demand for other sources, leading to minimum load challenges and potential grid stability issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-12-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity producers and consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Systemic operational issue. Its causes are: Low electricity demand, high rooftop PV output, and coal units operating at minimum load contributing to potential grid instability.

title: Demand in Victoria drops to a new lowest point on Sunday 31st December 2023, and AEMO flags possible intervention (Part 1?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand in Victoria drops to a new lowest point on Sunday 31st December 2023, and AEMO flags possible intervention (Part 1?)" posted at 31-12-2023 11:28:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reported a record low in market demand in Victoria, reaching 1,615 MW, below the secure threshold of 2,000 MW, mainly due to an increase in rooftop solar PV output. This posed a risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state from 11:00 to 14:00. Consequently, AEMO may need to intervene by directing scheduled generation, curtailing non-scheduled generation, or increasing operational demand to maintain system security; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (same day as the event). Its root_cause is: Increased rooftop solar PV output reducing market demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-12-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: N/A. Its cause type is: Systemic market condition. Its causes is: Rapid increase in rooftop solar PV generation reducing traditional market demand.

title: 25 days to go, until the initial return of Callide C3 (waiting another 141 days for Callide C4)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "25 days to go, until the initial return of Callide C3 (waiting another 141 days for Callide C4)" posted at 30-12-2023 05:13:00 PM is: The article discusses the anticipated return of the Callide C3 power generator on 24th January 2024, following an outage caused by cooling tower failure. Callide C4, which has been offline since an explosion on 25th May 2021, is expected to return by 19th May 2024. Recent developments include court proceedings initiated by Sev.en seeking further investigation into the explosion, potentially paving the way for claims against CS Energy and the Queensland government.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium to Long Term. Its root cause is: Explosion and cooling tower failure. Its accident scale is: Significant, affecting grid stability and supply. Its dates are: 30th December 2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Regional electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Technical and administrative issues. Its causes are: Cooling tower failure, Explosion at Callide C4, Delayed repair and maintenance.

title: Looking back at the high demand levels seen in the past two days in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking back at the high demand levels seen in the past two days in Queensland" posted at 30-12-2023 03:18:00 PM is: The news highlights the exceptionally high electricity demand experienced in Queensland on the 28th and 29th of December, 2023. Despite the cooler temperatures on the 30th of December leading to reduced demand, the days prior witnessed peak 'Market Demand' levels, approaching all-time highs, due to seasonal factors and disruptions from recent weather events. Notably, this demand surge occurred during a typically low-activity holiday period. The peak demands were mainly due to reduced solar contributions as the sun set and continued high consumption despite storm and flood-induced outages. The situation led to spot price volatility triggered by the limited supply options. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, spanning the affected days. Its root_cause is: Seasonal demand surge magnified by weather-related outages and reduced solar contributions after sunset. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-12-2023, 29-12-2023, and 30-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, particularly southeast and Far North regions. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland, with attention to those relying on solar and grid supply. Its cause type is: Demand-related. Its causes are: High demand during holiday period, storm and flood outages, reduced solar generation after peak sunlight, and resultant supply-demand imbalances.

title: Queensland electricity demand climbs higher still, on Friday 29th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland electricity demand climbs higher still, on Friday 29th December 2023" posted at 29-12-2023 05:36:00 PM is: The article discusses the rise in electricity demand in Queensland, reaching a peak of 9,773MW, surpassing the previous day's high of 9,753MW. Remarkably, this demand occurred during the typically low activity period between Christmas and New Year and was further complicated by power outages in several regions due to recent storms and flooding. Additionally, spot prices spiked to $14,936.02/MWh as demand increased with the decline in solar output. The supply was stretched further with the unplanned trip of the Tarong North coal-fired unit amid low wind production. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: High demand coinciding with reduced solar output and a generator trip. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 29-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-induced and operational. Its causes are: High demand due to holiday period, reduction in solar power availability, storms and flooding power outages, generator trip, and low wind production.

title: AEMO forecasts that tomorrow’s demand in QLD (i.e. Fri 29th Dec 2023) will be higher than today
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts that tomorrow’s demand in QLD (i.e. Fri 29th Dec 2023) will be higher than today" posted at 28-12-2023 07:12:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reports that electricity demand in Queensland is expected to peak significantly on 29th December 2023, following an already high demand on 28th December, attributed to extreme weather conditions. This surge is notable considering the typically low activity period between Christmas and New Year, suggesting continued strain on the electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. title: QLD ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 9,500MW on Thursday 28th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 9,500MW on Thursday 28th December 2023" posted at 28-12-2023 05:29:00 PM is: The electricity market demand in Queensland reached a significant peak of approximately 9,753MW on December 28th, 2023, amid high temperatures in Brisbane and reduced commercial and industrial activity due to the holiday period. This demand was notably close to breaking the all-time record of 10,119MW set in March 2022. The demand levels were influenced by approximately 50,000 homes in the Gold Coast area still being without power following Christmas storms. The situation also saw some price volatility resulting from the setting of solar generation and the introduction of various peaking power sources. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand driven by extreme weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential consumers, particularly in the Gold Coast area. Its cause type is: Natural event. Its causes are: High temperatures, Christmas storms leading to power outages, reduced industrial activity.

title: An initial exploration of QLD’s spike at 14:30 on Thursday 21st December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "An initial exploration of QLD’s spike at 14:30 on Thursday 21st December 2023" posted at 22-12-2023 02:12:00 PM is: On December 21, 2023, an unexpected price spike to $4,164.92/MWh occurred in Queensland at 14:30. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reported a non-credible contingency event involving the trip of circuit breakers in the QLD region. The main contributor to the price increase was identified as a reduction in the QNI interconnector's target flow. Lightning near Armidale caused AEMO to reclassify potential simultaneous trips of power lines as credible, invoking new constraints that affected the interconnector's capacity. The Middle Ridge circuit breaker trips also coincided with the event, although lightning around Armidale was the more significant factor. No load shedding was instructed, and the cause remains unknown.

Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 21/12/2023 until further notice. Its root_cause is: Lightning-induced credible contingency event and grid constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21/12/2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), and New South Wales (NSW1). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related and grid constraint. Its causes are: Lightning-induced credible contingency classification, trip of circuit breakers at Middle Ridge, QNI interconnector constraint, reclassification of power line trips due to weather conditions.

title: Very fast FCAS markets continue to grow
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very fast FCAS markets continue to grow" posted at 22-12-2023 01:53:00 PM is: The introduction of the 1-Second Raise and 1-Second Lower contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets since 9 October has witnessed significant growth in both the capacity and enabled volumes. As of late December, the RAISE1SEC capacity has exceeded 700MW, while LOWER1SEC capacity is approaching 600MW. Regular assessments by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) have progressively increased requirement caps to ensure adequate availability, with RAISE1SEC’s cap now set at 225MW and LOWER1SEC’s at 100MW. Availability generally surpasses requirement levels, but uncapped RAISE1SEC requirements remain high, highlighting the importance of these caps. A notable diurnal pattern is observed for RAISE1SEC requirements, influenced by PV generation and grid demand variations. Additionally, the Victorian Big Battery's commitment to the System Integrity Protection Scheme affects availability during specific periods, partly compensated by the Torrens Island Battery's deployment. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 9 October 2023 onwards. Its root_cause is: Implementation and adjustments in the FCAS markets. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9 October 2023 to 22 December 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Market adjustments and operational planning. Its causes are: Introduction of 1-Second Raise and Lower FCAS markets, adjustments to requirement caps by AEMO, diurnal pattern due to PV generation and grid demand, and changes in availability due to battery obligations.

title: Barron Gorge Hydro Station damaged and offline after flooding from Cyclone Japser in North Queensland
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Barron Gorge Hydro Station damaged and offline after flooding from Cyclone Japser in North Queensland" posted at 20-12-2023 03:23:00 PM is: The Barron Gorge Hydro power station in North Queensland has been rendered inoperable due to damage from flooding caused by ex-tropical Cyclone Jasper, specifically the destruction of the water intake structure at the Kuranda Weir. This damage has left both units offline since December 18, 2023, with operations expected to resume by July 1, 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Consultation on ‘Orderly Exit Management Framework Framework’ for coal units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Consultation on ‘Orderly Exit Management Framework Framework’ for coal units" posted at 16-12-2023 12:19:00 PM is: The New South Wales Government has released a consultation paper on an opt-in Orderly Exit Management (OEM) Framework designed to manage the transition of coal units in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This framework aims to extend the operational timeframe of thermal generators temporarily to secure energy reliability during the transition to cleaner technologies and an enhanced transmission network. While part of a Federal Government communication, the framework serves to ensure a structured energy market transition, with consultation open until 2 February 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Intentional policy development for market transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24 November 2023 (start of consultation) to 2 February 2024 (end of consultation), main date same as posted date. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy stakeholders and policymakers. Its cause type is: Policy/Administrative measure. Its causes are: Government initiative to manage coal unit transition, consultation for maintaining energy reliability and security.

title: AEMO forecast for LOR2 in NSW on Tuesday 19th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecast for LOR2 in NSW on Tuesday 19th December 2023" posted at 15-12-2023 10:11:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in New South Wales for Tuesday, 19th December 2023. This forecast indicates that between 2:30 PM and 4:00 PM on that day, the capacity reserve requirement will be 730 MW, with only 232 MW available, suggesting a significant shortfall. AEMO is seeking a market response to address this potential reserve shortfall, although it has not yet determined whether it will need to intervene directly. The situation follows a similar LOR2 condition the previous day, raising concerns for the summer period of 2023-24.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability . Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specific date of 19th December 2023). Its root_cause is: Anticipated insufficient reserve capacity in the grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-12-2023, 19-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast grid reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Anticipated reserve shortfall of 498 MW (capacity requirement of 730 MW vs. available reserve of 232 MW) on 19th December 2023.

title: AEMO released draft of 2024 Integrated System Plan … cue the media headlines
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO released draft of 2024 Integrated System Plan … cue the media headlines" posted at 15-12-2023 08:35:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published the draft of its 2024 Integrated System Plan (ISP), emphasizing the need for urgent investment in electricity infrastructure to ensure reliable and secure energy. The draft suggests that significant expansion in firming capacity from dispatchable storage, hydro, and gas-powered generation is necessary by 2050. The release has sparked extensive media coverage and public discussion, highlighting challenges such as the rapid reduction of coal power and the urgency in building replacement capacities. The draft ISP is open for stakeholder consultation until mid-February, with a final version expected by June 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium to Long-term (up to 2050). Its root cause is: Strategic planning for future energy infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-12-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Industry stakeholders, government, energy consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and planning. Its causes is: Release of draft ISP by AEMO emphasizing investment needs for future energy infrastructure, media reaction, stakeholder consultations.

title: Belated notice from the AEMO about RERT contracted for yesterday (Thu 14th Dec 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belated notice from the AEMO about RERT contracted for yesterday (Thu 14th Dec 2023)" posted at 15-12-2023 07:53:00 AM is: AEMO issued a belated market notice indicating that a reserve contract under the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) scheme was entered into for the New South Wales region on December 14, 2023, to maintain system reliability amidst high demand. However, no reserve contracts were activated during the specified period from 15:30 to 19:30 hours. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term, specific to December 14, 2023. Its root_cause is: high electricity demand and system reliability concerns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: System Demand Management. Its causes is: High demand for electricity prompting the need for reserve capacity to ensure system reliability.

title: Disallowed flow north on VIC1-NSW1 another significant factor in tight supply-demand in NSW + QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Disallowed flow north on VIC1-NSW1 another significant factor in tight supply-demand in NSW + QLD" posted at 14-12-2023 07:14:00 PM is: The article discusses complications arising from the VIC-NSW interconnector, which has been unable to flow north due to constraints, leading to tight supply-demand conditions in New South Wales and Queensland. With multiple constraint equations invoked, including 'N>>NIL_970_051', 'N>>NIL_4', and 'N>>NIL_998', the interconnector was effectively prevented from exporting electricity northward over a crucial period. This restriction could have contributed to the necessity of activating the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) mechanism. The inability to capture south-westerly generation efficiently to the Sydney load center is highlighted as a key issue; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Transmission constraints and negative settlement residues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Interconnector flow restriction. Its causes are: Negative settlement residues, multiple constraint equations, inability to export northward on the VIC-NSW interconnector.

title: Wind production ramps down in NSW, coincident with Increased Demand (and then Actual LOR2)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind production ramps down in NSW, coincident with Increased Demand (and then Actual LOR2)" posted at 14-12-2023 06:34:00 PM is: The report highlights a significant reduction in wind power generation in New South Wales (NSW), which contributed to the region reaching 'Actual LOR2' status due to increased electricity demand and other factors, such as restricted energy flow between Victoria and NSW. Between 15:45 and 18:25, wind production declined significantly from 1,463MW to 918MW, exacerbating the supply-demand gap in NSW. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural variability in wind generation. Its causes are: Rapid drop in wind power generation, increased electricity demand, restricted energy flow between regions.

title: Actual LOR2 in NSW from 17:30 on Thursday 14th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual LOR2 in NSW from 17:30 on Thursday 14th December 2023" posted at 14-12-2023 06:15:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in New South Wales due to increased market demand on 14 December 2023. This emergency reserve notice was issued due to the reserve capacity required being 690 MW, while the minimum available was only 678 MW. However, the condition was canceled shortly at 18:10 after market response proved sufficient to address the deficit; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 14 December 2023 but resolved within approximately 40 minutes. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational/system condition triggered by high demand. title: NSW ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 13,000MW – and IRPM drops – but prices stay moderate
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 13,000MW – and IRPM drops – but prices stay moderate" posted at 14-12-2023 06:02:00 PM is: In New South Wales (NSW), the electricity market demand has risen above 13,000MW, yet the spot prices remain moderate, contrary to earlier predictions. The Inter-regional Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) between New South Wales and Queensland has decreased to 14% due to constrained interconnectors preventing flow between VIC1-NSW1 regions. The highest point for the electricity price today reached $662.97/MWh.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector and demand pressure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland interconnected regions. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Grid/system operational constraints. Its causes is: High market demand, interconnector constraints due to grid stability equations, and moderate price forecasts.

title: NSW ‘Market Demand’ squeezes 12,500MW as price passes $500/MWh
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ squeezes 12,500MW as price passes $500/MWh" posted at 14-12-2023 04:54:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant market demand in New South Wales (NSW), where the electricity demand peaked at 12,464MW, leading to a spot price exceeding $500/MWh. Initially expected to reach 13,000MW, the demand forecast was revised down, and while a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) was initially warned, it was downgraded to LOR1, indicating a less severe than anticipated stress on the system; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its affected key users is: General electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: High demand driven by temperature/weather conditions. Its causes is: High electricity demand due to warm weather, leading to elevated spot prices and reserve condition warnings.

title: RERT negotiations underway in NSW … at the same time as the NSW spot price is negative and forecast spikes have disappeared
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT negotiations underway in NSW … at the same time as the NSW spot price is negative and forecast spikes have disappeared" posted at 14-12-2023 04:22:00 PM is: The article discusses the situation in the New South Wales electricity market where the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) was preparing to initiate Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) negotiations due to anticipated high demand on December 14, 2023. This occurred despite currently negative spot prices and a moderation of forecast price spikes after sunset. classified_content: The summary of the news "Generator Compliance Management – Is it the “monster under the bed”?" posted at 11-12-2023 12:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the importance of compliance management with Generator Performance Standards (GPS) for large-scale electricity generation assets. It emphasizes the recent focus by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) on enforcing compliance with national electricity rules, resulting in significant penalties for non-compliance. The dynamics of technical non-compliance, such as inadequate processes and inadvertence, can lead to severe financial and reputational consequences for power generators. The article highlights various instances of penalties imposed on companies like AGL Energy Ltd and Hornsdale Power Reserve for breaches related to compliance with dispatch instructions and frequency control ancillary services. The discussion includes risks related to non-compliance, such as potential damage to investor confidence and project viability, and suggests proactive compliance management as a strategic approach. This is the first instance of such a peak since July 24, 2023. Both the New South Wales and Queensland regions largely contributed to this surge in demand, with NSW reaching 11,673MW and QLD reaching 8,728MW. title: Price volatility also in NSW on Friday 8th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility also in NSW on Friday 8th December 2023" posted at 08-12-2023 04:07:00 PM is: The article reports significant price spikes in NSW on 8th December 2023, occurring at 10:05 AM ($14,248.59/MWh) and 14:50 PM ($10,485.97/MWh) due to various transmission constraints and drops in wind output. The constraints impacted interconnector flows and led to a reduction in wind power generation, contributing to the extreme price levels. The constrained units include BOCOROCK, GULLRGWF, and COLWF, and the outages on the Ravine to Yass line invoked constraint equations, exacerbating the price spikes. Future demand spikes are anticipated as market demand might peak above 30,000MW later in the day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Likely same day as the spikes occurred and possibly extending due to ongoing transmission constraints. title: Case Study (Part 2) of Low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (Part 2) of Low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023" posted at 07-12-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses a significant event during the first week of July 2023, when the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia experienced sustained low levels of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) penetration. During July 3rd and 4th, VRE supplied less than 10% of total demand more than half of the time due to low solar and wind production, exacerbated by increased underlying demand. Cloud cover and low wind speeds across eastern Australia contributed to reduced solar irradiance and wind generation, highlighting the vulnerability of VRE-dependent systems during such 'Dunkelflaute' conditions. While forecasts anticipated different outcomes, actual VRE availability was significantly lower, stressing the need for system planners to address these challenges. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: July 3rd and 4th, 2023. Its root cause is: Extended cloud cover and low wind speeds impacting solar and wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: July 3rd and 4th, 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, particularly eastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Low solar irradiance due to cloud cover, reduced wind speeds due to a high-pressure system.

title: Return to service for Callide C3 delayed by 17 days (now to 24th January 2024)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Return to service for Callide C3 delayed by 17 days (now to 24th January 2024)" posted at 06-12-2023 03:59:00 PM is: The return-to-service for the Callide C3 unit has been postponed by 17 days, now expected on January 24, 2024, due to various factors including recent rain, hot weather, resource availability issues, COVID-19 impact, and global supply chain disruptions attributed partially to international humanitarian efforts and domestic challenges in India. This delay brings the timeline closer to the expected high-demand summer period. The investigation into Callide C4's failure by Dr. Sean Brady has no new updates. CS Energy administers the units, and both Callide C3 and C4 remain key focal points in the electricity market due to their prolonged outages following incidents since 2021 for C4 and 2022 for C3. Its cause type is: Operational delays due to infrastructure and external challenges. Its causes are: Cooling tower failure, adverse weather conditions, COVID-19, and global supply chain issues.

title: Case Study – Severe Weather Challenges Day-Ahead Forecasts for 7, 8, 9 September 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study – Severe Weather Challenges Day-Ahead Forecasts for 7, 8, 9 September 2023" posted at 06-12-2023 10:00:00 AM is: The article examines the impact of severe weather conditions from September 7th to 9th, 2023, on electricity forecasts in south-eastern Australia. During this period, strong winds and storms associated with a low-pressure system disrupted forecast accuracy for operational demand, wind, and solar power availability. Specifically, on September 8th, operational demand exceeded forecasts by over 2,000 MW due to lower-than-expected rooftop PV performance and colder temperatures increasing demand for heating. Wind and solar availability showed significant deviations from forecasts over the three days, influenced by local weather patterns and cloud cover. The case study highlights the challenges in predicting electricity market behavior under extreme weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during and slightly after the event from September 7-9, 2023. Its affected region is: South-eastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market operators and consumers in the NEM (National Electricity Market). Its cause type is: Natural (weather-related). Its causes are: Severe weather including strong winds, storms, lower-than-expected rooftop PV, and colder temperatures increasing demand.

title: Worth a read – ESIG Report ‘Weather Dataset Needs for Planning and Analyzing Modern Power Systems’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Worth a read – ESIG Report ‘Weather Dataset Needs for Planning and Analyzing Modern Power Systems’" posted at 06-12-2023 08:43:00 AM is: The article discusses insights from an ESIG Webinar, highlighting the increasing dependence of modern power systems, such as the North American grid and the NEM, on weather patterns. Key points from Justin Sharp include the necessity for better planning to mitigate variability, challenges with modeling weather data, and the complexity of wind and solar forecasting. The focus was on needing accurate datasets to assist in managing the uncertainties inherent in renewable energy generation and its impact on the power grid.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its cause type is: Data and modelling challenges in weather-dependent power systems. title: Brief period of spot volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday evening 5th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief period of spot volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday evening 5th December 2023" posted at 05-12-2023 06:58:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a brief period of spot price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on the evening of December 5, 2023. This volatility was linked to elevated demand levels and logistical constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, which formed an 'Economic Island' and resulted in an Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of less than 15%. Additionally, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) low reserve condition for NSW. A prior dispatch process glitch was noted as contributing to the issue. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector and AEMO dispatch process glitch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, elevated demand levels, and a glitch in AEMO's dispatch process.

title: Rooftop Solar is crushing* the returns for Large-Scale VRE
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Rooftop Solar is crushing* the returns for Large-Scale VRE" posted at 05-12-2023 10:30:00 AM is: The report discusses how rooftop solar installations have significantly affected the returns of large-scale Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) generators for the third quarter of 2023, highlighting the stark difference between Volume-Weighted Average (VWA) prices and Time-Weighted Average (TWA) prices across regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The report points out the particularly low VWA prices for large solar farms compared to TWA prices and notes the occurrence of negative VWA prices in some regions. This trend underscores the challenges of the 'Solar Correlation Penalty' and the inadequacy of the Capacity Factor metric for comparing solar farm performance. Wind farms faced similar issues, though with a less severe impact, due to the 'Wind Correlation Penalty', particularly in South Australia and Victoria. The discussion also includes varying opinions on incentivizing rooftop PV and the implications of potential policy changes. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing in Q3 2023. Its root_cause is: Rooftop solar installations affecting VRE prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2023. Its affected region is: Nationwide across the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Large-scale VRE operators. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Dominance of rooftop PV installations, lower VWA prices for large-scale VREs, solar and wind correlation penalties.
