title: SA Islanding: Managing solar on the island
author: Blake Ashhton
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA Islanding: Managing solar on the island" posted at 01-12-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges and strategies involved in managing South Australia's electricity grid as it becomes "islanded" during high-rooftop-solar-generation periods, especially when disconnected from the Victorian Heywood interconnector. Notably, during a severe storm event in November 2022 that knocked out the interconnector for nearly a week, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had to curtail solar power to maintain grid stability as variable renewable energy peaked at over 91%. SA Power Networks employed several measures, including directly instructing connected solar generators to shut down, using the government's Relevant Agent arrangements for remote disconnection, and adjusting substation voltages to manage older systems. The ongoing development of the Flexible Exports option aims to optimize energy flows by employing Dynamic Operating Envelopes, enhancing SA's capacity to handle high renewable energy penetration. The state's plans for the new SA/NSW transmission interconnector to be completed by 2026 are also mentioned as a future safeguard. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the outage and long-term with the introduction of new measures. Its root_cause is: Severe storm causing the loss of the Heywood interconnector. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in South Australia, especially those with rooftop solar installations. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Severe storm event leading to the loss of the Heywood interconnector, resulting in the requirement for grid stability management measures like solar curtailment.

title: Do we really need Eraring when we’ll have 5,500MW of batteries by 2026?
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "Do we really need Eraring when we’ll have 5,500MW of batteries by 2026?" posted at 30-11-2023 03:00:00 PM is: The article discusses the future of the Eraring Power Station in the context of increasing battery storage capacities and renewable energy sources in New South Wales (NSW). With the NSW Government's Capacity Investment Scheme awarding contracts for 1,075MW of batteries and 2,980MWh of demand response projects, along with upcoming wind and solar projects, the article argues that the closure of the Eraring Power Station might not have as detrimental an impact on the state's power supply as previously feared. New renewable capacities are projected to outpace the generation lost from closing both the Eraring and Liddell stations, addressing concerns over supply gaps and potential blackouts. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: By 2026, when the new battery and renewable capacities are operational. Root_cause is: Transition from coal to renewable energy sources and battery storage. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates is: 30-11-2023. Affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Affected key users is: General electricity consumers in NSW. Cause type is: Energy transition policy. Causes is: Closure of Eraring and Liddell power stations, government policies on renewable energy, delays in new transmission projects, introduction of new battery storage capacities.

title: What’s happening around Wagga?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s happening around Wagga?" posted at 30-11-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses ongoing issues within the National Electricity Market (NEM) related to counter-intuitive energy flows between New South Wales (NSW) and Victoria. The core problem centers on consistent southward flows of electricity from more expensive NSW into cheaper Victoria, driven by limitations in the transmission infrastructure, especially around Wagga, where increased solar generation is affecting the capability of the existing network to manage power flows securely. These constraints, particularly related to the 051 line and the 132kV network, result in significant negative financial settlements termed "inter-regional settlement residues" (IRSR). The article suggests potential solutions such as network upgrades or implementing a "runback scheme" to curtail generation during grid stress, but notes that these issues may persist in the absence of substantial infrastructure enhancements. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing. Its root_cause is: Transmission constraints around Wagga. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 3 November 2023 (specific examples given). Its affected region is: Southern New South Wales and Northern Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Structural and operational inefficiencies. Its causes are: Increased solar generation around Wagga, outdated transmission network capacity, 051 line constraint, and counterintuitive dispatch outcomes.

title: Thunderstorms in South Australia impact ~30,000 electricity customers on Tuesday 28th November 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Thunderstorms in South Australia impact ~30,000 electricity customers on Tuesday 28th November 2023" posted at 29-11-2023 02:51:00 PM is: Super-cell thunderstorms hit Adelaide and surrounding regions in the early hours of Tuesday, resulting in severe power outages affecting approximately 30,000 customers due to unplanned outages and trips in the electricity network. Significant market events coupled with the weather conditions led to a spike in the energy price ceiling of $16,600/MWh as system constraints clamped energy exports from Victoria. By the evening, only 1,000 customers were still without power. Extreme weather conditions, confirmed by the ABC and Weatherzone, included flash flooding, with over 43,000 lightning strikes recorded, marking the wettest November day in 18 years for Adelaide. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact on the same day, persisting potentially for days. Its root_cause is: Super-cell thunderstorms causing equipment failures and network strain. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting approximately 30,000 customers. Its dates are: 28th November 2023 for the main events. Its affected region is: South East South Australia, including Adelaide and surrounding towns. Its affected key users are: Approximately 30,000 electricity customers. Its cause type is: Natural event (extreme weather). Its causes are: Super-cell thunderstorms, flash flooding, and multiple network trips and unplanned outages.

title: Federal Government announces expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme on 23rd November 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Federal Government announces expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme on 23rd November 2023" posted at 23-11-2023 02:01:00 PM is: The Federal Government, led by Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen, has announced a significant expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) along with the National Energy Transformation Partnership (NETP), aiming to transition from the current Renewable Energy Target scheme set to end in 2030. The expanded CIS will introduce 9 GW of dispatchable and 23 GW of variable capacity, totaling 32 GW nationwide, with half being subject to state negotiations. Various industry leaders have expressed support, noting the potential positive impacts on investment, electricity prices, and reliability, though some have flagged risks and areas for further work. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, likely through and beyond 2030. Its root cause is: Government policy changes aimed at supporting renewable energy investment. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-11-2023. Its affected region is: National (Australia). Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and investors. Its cause type is: Strategic policy decision. Its causes are: Expiration of the Renewable Energy Target Scheme, the need for increased renewable capacity, and the Federal Government's renewable goals.

title: AEMO holds 2023-24 Summer Readiness Briefing on November 13th 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO holds 2023-24 Summer Readiness Briefing on November 13th 2023" posted at 16-11-2023 11:10:00 AM is: The AEMO conducted its annual Summer Readiness Industry Briefing on November 13th, 2023, focusing on electricity and gas system readiness in anticipation of the 2023/24 summer. Presentations covered a range of topics including weather forecasts, network readiness, and bushfire preparations, highlighting heightened risks of blackouts due to extreme power demand driven by a strong El Nino. The session emphasized the role of renewables and battery storage in managing potential electricity grid stress. Media reports expressed confidence in the energy system's ability to withstand challenges if current plans succeed. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2023/24. Its root_cause is: Anticipated extreme weather and heightened power demand driven by El Nino. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 16-11-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural and market dynamics. Its causes is: Strong El Nino conditions, increased electricity demand, power system preparations.

title: Case Study (part 1) of Aggregate Scheduled Target on Friday 8th September 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (part 1) of Aggregate Scheduled Target on Friday 8th September 2023" posted at 14-11-2023 12:29:00 PM is: The article discusses a notable event in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during Q3 2023, specifically a significant 5-minute increase in the Aggregate Scheduled Target (+960MW) between 13:35 and 13:40 on 8th September 2023. This spike is connected with a notable drop in the aggregate output from large solar units in New South Wales (NSW) within a 10-minute window, which likely caused fully scheduled units to dispatch more electricity. The NSW region price experienced a decline during this period, dropping to just below $0/MWh by 13:40 due to these market dynamics; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the day of the event). Its root_cause is: Drop in solar output leading to adjustments in scheduled electricity dispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 8th September 2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational adjustment due to solar output variance. Its causes are: Drop in large solar output, resulting in dispatch adjustments to meet electricity demand.

title: Forecast low System Strength of concern in NSW for Tuesday 14th November 2023 … first time ever?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast low System Strength of concern in NSW for Tuesday 14th November 2023 … first time ever?" posted at 13-11-2023 03:54:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a Market Notice indicating a possible intervention in the New South Wales (NSW) electricity region to manage power system security due to forecasted low system strength. The synchronous generating units expected to be online might be inadequate to maintain a secure operating state on the evening of November 14, 2023, prompting potential directions for additional units to operate. This scenario is more common in South Australia and sometimes Victoria, but unusual for NSW. Current inertia levels in NSW are low, and upcoming unplanned maintenance outages for key units might be contributing to the situation; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term, specifically on November 14, 2023. Its root_cause is: Insufficient system strength due to low inertia and potential inadequate synchronisation of generating units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-11-2023, with significant impact expected by 14-11-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and grid operators in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational/System security. Its causes are: Forecasted inadequate synchronisation of synchronous generating units, low current inertia levels, and upcoming unplanned maintenance outages.

title: Victoria’s ‘Market Demand’ low point drops further–on Sunday 12th November 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria’s ‘Market Demand’ low point drops further–on Sunday 12th November 2023" posted at 12-11-2023 11:14:00 AM is: The article reports a significant drop in 'Market Demand' in Victoria on November 12, 2023. This event marks a further decline from the previously noted low point on September 16, 2023. 'Market Demand' reached a minimum of 1,724.66 MW, whereas 'Operational Demand' was notably higher at 2,119 MW for the half-hour ending at 13:00. The situation highlights varying demand measures used by AEMO to assess electricity market dynamics in Victoria. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact on electricity balance and potential pricing. Its root_cause is: Low market demand due to varying operational measures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12th November 2023. Its affected region is: Victorian region. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market operational measures. Its causes are: Low market demand, comparison of market and operational demand measures.

title: A snapshot of Eraring unit 4 on Thursday afternoon 9th Nov 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A snapshot of Eraring unit 4 on Thursday afternoon 9th Nov 2023" posted at 10-11-2023 08:00:00 PM is: The news discusses the impacts of ramp rates observed at Eraring unit 4 on the evening of 9th November 2023, highlighting the issue of spot price volatility in South Australia. It uses a snapshot from the 'Unit Dashboard' widget in ez2view to illustrate how ramp rates can affect available capacity in bid operations; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Limitations in ramp rates affecting the generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9th November 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and operators. Its cause type is: Technical limitation. Its causes are: Ramp rate constraints impacting bid capacity.

title: Spot price volatility in South Australia on Thursday evening 9th Nov 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in South Australia on Thursday evening 9th Nov 2023" posted at 10-11-2023 12:31:00 PM is: The article discusses significant spot price volatility in South Australia on the evening of November 9, 2023. The interconnectors, 'V::S_SETB_TBSE_2' and 'V^^SML_NIL_3', were constrained, limiting power flow westward on Heywood and Murraylink, respectively. Contributing factors included the absence of solar generation since nighttime, and lower-than-expected wind generation. These conditions led to a shift of generation volume into higher-priced bid bands, highlighting a pivotal shift in market dynamics during this period. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate effects on the evening of November 9, 2023). Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnectors and low renewable generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9th November 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Grid constraints and low renewable generation. Its causes are: Constraint equations limiting power flow on Heywood and Murraylink, lower-than-expected wind generation, and no solar generation at night.

title: Media Release = ‘Capacity Investment Scheme supports NSW to deliver 1 GW of cleaner, cheaper, more reliable energy’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Media Release = ‘Capacity Investment Scheme supports NSW to deliver 1 GW of cleaner, cheaper, more reliable energy’" posted at 09-11-2023 06:00:00 PM is: The Capacity Investment Scheme announced by the NSW Department of Energy aims to bolster energy reliability and cost-effectiveness through six successful projects that collectively will provide 1 GW of power, featuring advancements like Orana, Liddell, and Smithfield batteries, as well as virtual power plants. All projects are set for commercial operations by December 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: By December 2025. Its root_cause is: Policy implementation to enhance energy capacity and reliability in NSW. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22nd November 2023 (publication date of the media release). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy-driven. Its causes are: Implementation of projects within the Capacity Investment Scheme to increase energy capacity and reliability.

title: Bigger or better: Are newer wind farms outperforming older ones?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bigger or better: Are newer wind farms outperforming older ones?" posted at 09-11-2023 11:56:00 AM is: The article examines the anticipation that advancements in wind farm technology will lead to higher capacity factors, thus improving efficiency over time. It discusses the CSIRO’s projections of increased future capacity factors for wind farms, yet analysis shows no consistent trend in improvement over the last two decades, with various factors like site scarcity and curtailment counteracting technological gains. Thus, newer wind farms are not necessarily outperforming older ones in terms of capacity factors. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term projections up to 2050. Its root_cause is: Technological advancements in wind energy with countering efficiency factors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: September 11, 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and efficiency analysis. Its causes are: Technological improvements, site scarcity, curtailment due to economic reasons, network constraints, and wind diversity.

title: Case Study (part 1) of low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (part 1) of low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023" posted at 06-11-2023 12:48:00 PM is: The article provides an analysis of the low percentage of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) supply across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on July 3rd and 4th, 2023. During these two days, VRE supply reached daily minimum levels of 3.4% and 3.8%, respectively, with maximum levels only at 27.1% and 20%. These values are notably lower than usual, with over half of the dispatch intervals recording VRE supply under 10% of the total. The focus on VRE percentage rather than total renewable percentage is emphasized to better understand challenges in the energy transition. The article suggests referring to the GenInsights 2023 Q3 report for a more comprehensive discussion on these metrics.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the 3rd and 4th of July 2023. Its root_cause is: Low VRE production due to circumstances within those days. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-07-2023 and 04-07-2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide (covering multiple areas within the National Electricity Market in Australia). Its affected key users are: NEM stakeholders and energy market participants. Its cause type is: Market and operational analysis. Its causes are: Low percentage of VRE supply across NEM, low VRE production on specified days.

title: No, South Australia is *not* exporting more electricity than it imports…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "No, South Australia is *not* exporting more electricity than it imports…" posted at 05-11-2023 02:22:00 PM is: The article debunks a claim that South Australia is exporting more electricity than it imports, highlighting that since January 2021, the region has primarily been importing electricity from Victoria. The narrative emphasizes changes in the National Electricity Market and mentions the potential impact of Project Energy Connect, which aims to connect regions currently reliant on imports. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since January 2021. Its root_cause is: Misinterpretation of energy flow data and energy transition dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-11-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and policy misunderstanding. Its causes are: Inaccurate claims about energy export-import balances and changes in energy infrastructure plans.

title: Bouldercombe BESS charges for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday 3rd November 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bouldercombe BESS charges for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday 3rd November 2023" posted at 04-11-2023 12:19:00 PM is: The Bouldercombe Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Queensland experienced controlled charging activity for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday, November 3rd, 2023. This charging event occurred as part of a broader initiative to commence final commissioning tests and operations following prior fire-related disruptions. Tesla had conducted a root cause analysis (RCA) remotely and subsequently through physical inspections on their affected Megapack unit. Genex Power indicated that 38 out of 40 Megapack units would be utilized for the recommencement of operations, anticipated by mid-November. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Prior fire incident and subsequent diagnostics/repairs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-11-2023, with related announcements noted from 30-10-2023 and 13-10-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical/operational issue. Its causes are: Fire incident, remote diagnostics, physical inspection, commissioning preparations.

title: Case Study (part 1) of large collective under-performance for Semi-Scheduled assets on Friday 18th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (part 1) of large collective under-performance for Semi-Scheduled assets on Friday 18th August 2023" posted at 02-11-2023 04:33:00 PM is: The news discusses an analysis of a large collective under-performance by Semi-Scheduled assets in the electricity market on August 18, 2023. It looks into the 'Aggregate Raw Off-Target' metric and reveals dispatch errors throughout the day. This incident is assessed in the context of quarterly reports from AER and AEMO for Q3 2023, which provide broader insights into market dynamics.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specifically on the date mentioned). Its root_cause is: Dispatch errors among Semi-Scheduled assets. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-08-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled asset operators. Its cause type is: Operational inefficiency. Its causes are: Large collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled assets, dispatch errors observed throughout the day.

title: An updated look at Kogan Creek performance in the past 60 days (re industrial action, bushfires, ambient temp, ez2view enhancements, and more)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An updated look at Kogan Creek performance in the past 60 days (re industrial action, bushfires, ambient temp, ez2view enhancements, and more)" posted at 01-11-2023 07:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the performance of Kogan Creek over the past 60 days, highlighting various factors such as the threat of industrial action, high ambient temperatures affecting operations especially since Kogan Creek is dry-cooled, and recent physical problems with the plant. Despite numerous references to ambient temperature issues, there was no significant industrial action or bushfire impact on Kogan Creek's operations. Observations include a forced outage on 17th October and recent output limitations due to physical problems, though no future outages are scheduled. Overall, these developments have been analyzed using the enhancements in the 'Bids & Offers' and 'Generator Outages' widgets in the ez2view tool, providing insights into the status of coal fleet units.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root cause is: High ambient temperatures and physical problems. Its accident scale is: Plant-specific. Its dates are: 01-11-2023 and the past 60 days preceding this date. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market analysts and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Operational and environmental. Its causes are: Industrial action threats, ambient temperatures affecting dry-cooled systems, and recent plant physical problems.

title: Challenges with a global-only FCAS requirement
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Challenges with a global-only FCAS requirement" posted at 01-11-2023 09:08:00 AM is: The article discusses the Fast Frequency Response (FCAS) market that commenced on October 9, 2023, highlighting transitional arrangements and the current state of requirements, specifically the Basslink outage from October 28 to November 2, 2023. This outage emphasizes the limitations in managing contingencies between the mainland and Tasmania, prompting considerations for local VF FCAS requirements. Additionally, updates to FCAS constraints are anticipated to account for Basslink's status. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Current and near-term adjustment phase until November 2, 2023, and continued impact depending on further outages and adjustments post that date. Its root_cause is: Basslink outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: October 28, 2023, to November 2, 2023. Its affected region is: Mainland and Tasmania NEM regions. Its affected key users are: Energy operators and market participants within the NEM. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Basslink outage, transitional arrangements in the FCAS market, and current limitations in contingency management.

title: Very fast raise service to be expanded
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Very fast raise service to be expanded" posted at 31-10-2023 04:03:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has announced an increase in the cap on dispatch of Very Fast Raise (R1) Contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) from 50 MW to 100 MW, effective on 6 November 2023. This follows a review of the 1-second FCAS markets and aims to ensure sufficient market capacity and avoid supply shortages. The cap for Very Fast Lower (L1) Contingency FCAS will remain at 50 MW. Additional system-normal contingency risks will also be considered in the FCAS determination. Prices for Raise 1 Sec have been noted to increase to $30-$40/MWh from the current level of around $20/MWh as integration progresses. Further changes include constraint updates related to the Basslink configuration. 

Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Starting on 6 November 2023. The root cause is: AEMO's decision to increase the requirements for Very Fast Raise (R1) services after reviewing market operations. The accident scale is: Unknown (though no specific accident is mentioned, the notice deals with preventive measures). The dates are: 31-10-2023, with changes implemented from 6-11-2023. The affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM). The affected key users are: Participants in the FCAS market, particularly those invested in or relying on 1-second response services. The cause type is: Market operations decision. The causes are: Review of FCAS caps and registered capacity, ensuring the sufficiency of market capacity to meet new requirement volumes, and maintaining the integrity of system operations.

title: AGL (Bayswater and Loy Yang A) fined for inability to supply FCAS services
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AGL (Bayswater and Loy Yang A) fined for inability to supply FCAS services" posted at 31-10-2023 08:42:00 AM is: AGL's Bayswater and Loy Yang A power stations were fined several million dollars for failing to provide Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) after bidding and being accepted by the National Electricity Market Dispatch Engine (NEMDE). The penalties were imposed following a Federal Court case, with the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) announcing the $6 million fines for not supplying the promised backup electricity services. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term. Its root_cause is: inability to supply agreed FCAS services. Its accident scale is: financial penalty imposed. Its dates are: 30th October 2023 (Federal Court listing date). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: electricity consumers relying on grid stability. Its cause type is: regulatory failure to meet FCAS commitments. Its causes are: failure of Bayswater and Loy Yang A stations to deliver promised FCAS services as per their accepted bids.

title: Minimum Market Demand in NSW drops lower still, on Sunday 29th October 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Minimum Market Demand in NSW drops lower still, on Sunday 29th October 2023" posted at 29-10-2023 10:52:00 AM is: The article reports a new record low in market demand for electricity in New South Wales, which has decreased to 3,664MW at the 11:45 dispatch interval on 29th October 2023. This decline follows a previous low recorded three weeks prior on 8th October 2023. The decreased demand coincides with increased rooftop PV installation and favorable weather for solar energy. Discussions at the recent All Energy 2023 conference highlighted potential policy changes to further increase rooftop PV adoption. The AEMO, on 30th October, confirmed the low demand trend via LinkedIn.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, influenced by time of day and ongoing rooftop PV installations. Root_cause is: Increase in rooftop solar PV leading to reduced grid demand and conditions conducive to solar generation.; Accident scale is: Not applicable. Dates are: 29-10-2023. Affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Affected key users are: Electricity consumers and PV system owners. Cause type is: Market and policy-related. Causes are: High solar PV generation, favorable weather, policy discussions on further adoption of PV systems.

title: Brief period of ‘Actual LOR2’ in the SA region Friday evening 27th October 2023 with low wind and constrained imports
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief period of ‘Actual LOR2’ in the SA region Friday evening 27th October 2023 with low wind and constrained imports" posted at 27-10-2023 08:57:00 PM is: On the evening of October 27, 2023, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in South Australia due to low wind output and constrained imports from Victoria. The condition was declared at 19:49 NEM time and was called off shortly after 20:00, lasting slightly over 30 minutes. Factors leading to this were reduced scheduled capacity and import capability alongside underlying conditions of low variable renewable energy (VRE) output and import limitation over the Heywood interconnector due to a transmission line outage.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: A combination of low wind energy output, constrained import capabilities, and scheduled capacity reductions. Its accident scale is: Localized to the South Australia region. Its dates is: 27-10-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the South Australia region. Its cause type is: Operational and physical constraints. Its causes is: Low renewable energy output and import capacity limitations due to transmission constraints.

title: Cumulative price in SA goes negative on October 26th 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cumulative price in SA goes negative on October 26th 2023" posted at 27-10-2023 10:53:00 AM is: The news reports that for the first time, the cumulative price in South Australia has turned negative, recording a significant drop to -$13,114 following its initial negative interval on October 26th, 2023. This trend highlights a period of prolonged low and negative pricing, which is rare in the history of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Such a situation indicates potential risks and signals an extended duration of low wholesale prices that could affect market stability. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Prolonged low and negative pricing in the South Australian market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: October 26, 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic/Market conditions. Its causes are: Extended period of low and negative spot prices in South Australia.

title: Demand Response as Market Participant: Square Peg in Round Hole?
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand Response as Market Participant: Square Peg in Round Hole?" posted at 24-10-2023 03:24:00 PM is: The article reviews the impact and current state of the Wholesale Demand Response (WDR) mechanism in the National Electricity Market (NEM) two years after its implementation. The mechanism, introduced on 24 October 2021, aims to allow large electricity consumers to sell reduced consumption into the wholesale electricity market. While the mechanism had a promising start, the second year saw a 65% drop in dispatched volumes, indicating a need for further adaptation and growth. Factors affecting WDR include competing demand response opportunities, atypical weather conditions, and improved coal fleet reliability reducing price volatility. Despite these challenges, WDR continues to present a long-term potential to balance supply-demand issues and lower electricity prices.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing, as WDR mechanisms evolve over time. Its root cause is: Limited adaptation and competing market opportunities. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24 October 2021 - 23 October 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Large commercial and industrial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: System and market design issues. Its causes are: Limited adaptation of WDR, competing opportunities for demand response, improved coal fleet reliability, atypical weather conditions, and eligibility barriers.

title: On Mon 23rd October, the AEMO publishes Quarterly Energy Dynamics for 2023 Q3
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes Quarterly Energy Dynamics for 2023 Q3" posted at 23-10-2023 07:00:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released its Quarterly Energy Dynamics report for Q3 2023, highlighting significant shifts in energy supply sources. Renewables, including wind, solar, and hydro, increased their share of total electricity supply to 38.9%, driven partly by the closure of the Liddell Power Station. This shift contributed to a decrease in black coal usage by 3.4% and gas usage by 2.3%, although brown coal usage saw a slight increase due to fewer unplanned outages. The report noted ongoing developments in new energy projects, with 1.7 GW of new projects progressing through registration and 2.1 GW commissioning to full output, reflecting a robust expansion in renewable energy infrastructure. The report also discussed the phenomenon of negative pricing events, posing increased risks for market participants, which could impact future energy contracts. Media coverage from various outlets emphasized the price decreases in wholesale electricity due to the renewable surge but cautioned about impending high prices due to other factors. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term, as it highlights ongoing energy market developments and potential future pricing implications. Its root_cause is: Transition from fossil fuels to renewable energies, and the closure of Liddell Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-10-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants (retailers, generators, consumers). Its cause type is: Market dynamics and policy shifts. Its causes are: Increased renewable energy contributions, closure of coal-fired power station, decreased demand for coal and gas, and occurrences of negative pricing events.

title: On Thu 19th October, the AER published Quarterly Review for 2023 Q3
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "On Thu 19th October, the AER published Quarterly Review for 2023 Q3" posted at 21-10-2023 04:50:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator's (AER) Wholesale Markets Quarterly report for Q3 2023 highlights a drop in wholesale electricity and gas prices due to mild winter conditions leading to lower energy demand. The report also notes that two wind farms, two solar farms, and two batteries started operating, adding 800 megawatts of capacity, yet emphasizes the need for more capacity to replace retiring coal plants over the next decade. The media coverage referenced in the report reflects on the sharp fall in energy prices from record low electricity demand and the role of rooftop solar in reducing wholesale prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: July to September 2023. Its root_cause is: Mild winter leading to lower electricity demand and prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-10-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers. Its cause type is: Natural and market-driven. Its causes are: Lower energy demand from mild weather and record rooftop solar output.

title: Brief loss of load around Townsville in the early hours of Saturday morning 21st October 2023
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2023-10-21 Lost Load in QLD Ross Zone
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief loss of load around Townsville in the early hours of Saturday morning 21st October 2023" posted at 21-10-2023 11:00:00 AM is: An unexpected loss of electrical load occurred in the Townsville area, affecting the Ross Zone in Queensland due to the tripping of the Ross No 2 275kV Bus at 01:33 on 21st October 2023. This resulted in a temporary bulk electrical load disconnection of 218 MW. Although no load shedding was instructed by AEMO, the event led to an interruption mainly impacting several large industrial users including Glencore’s Copper Refineries and the Sun Metals zinc smelter. The cause of this non-credible contingency event is unidentified but was resolved with the system returning to normalcy by 02:08. Power was gradually restored by 04:50, although not reaching pre-event consumption levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, less than a day. Its root_cause is: Unknown technical fault of the Ross No 2 275kV Bus. Its accident scale is: Localized within the Townsville area (Ross Zone). Its dates are: 21/10/2023. Its affected region is: Townsville area, Ross Zone, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Large industrial energy users including Glencore’s Copper Refineries and the Sun Metals zinc smelter. Its cause type is: Technical/Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Tripping of the Ross No 2 275kV Bus, leading to a non-credible contingency event with an unresolved initial cause.

title: Two pieces of information (about Eraring Power Station) from the Origin Energy 2023 AGM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two pieces of information (about Eraring Power Station) from the Origin Energy 2023 AGM" posted at 19-10-2023 10:42:00 AM is: The article details highlights from Origin Energy's 2023 Annual General Meeting, particularly focusing on the Eraring Power Station. This includes the power station's low forced outage rate of under 5% over the past year and ongoing discussions regarding the potential early closure of the station, slated for 2025. The meeting highlighted exceptional performance metrics for Eraring, while also addressing strategic adjustments in preparation for the transition to cleaner energy sources. Negotiations with the New South Wales government on delaying the closure date were also noted, alongside the potential implications these have on reliability and market dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing until the proposed closure in August 2025. Its root_cause is: Transition from coal-fired energy to renewable sources and market reliability concerns. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 18-10-2023 and onward discussions regarding closure until August 2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Large-scale electricity users and residents reliant on coal power. Its cause type is: Policy and operational transition. Its causes are: Low forced outage rates; negotiations over closure dates; transition to renewable energy and market reliability.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Research Paper into solar resource intermittency in future (in a climate changed world)" posted at 19-10-2023 08:13:00 AM is: The article discusses a recent study addressing the effects of climate change on solar and wind energy patterns in Australia, emphasizing solar resource intermittency and reliability. title: AER issues Infringement Notice to CS Energy relating to long-term operating Callide C without an exemption … including on 24th May 2021
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER issues Infringement Notice to CS Energy relating to long-term operating Callide C without an exemption … including on 24th May 2021" posted at 18-10-2023 04:08:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has issued an infringement notice to CS Energy for allegedly operating the Callide C power station without regulatory approval prior to the explosion on 25th May 2021. This incident led to the Callide C4 unit being offline, resulting in one of the longest outages in the history of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Although the lack of an exemption was identified on 24th May 2021, it was not directly linked to the explosion the following day, highlighting coincidental timing rather than causation. Nonetheless, CS Energy is facing a fine as reported by Giles Parkinson and others. The CS Energy, owning Callide C jointly, acknowledges their commitment to market compliance amidst the extended outage and regulatory issues. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with significant impact expected until the unit is returned to service. Its root_cause is: Alleged operation without regulatory approval and subsequent explosion. Its accident scale is: Large-scale, affecting the power supply within the NEM. Its dates are: 18-10-2023 (publication date), 24th May 2021 (identification of regulatory issue), 25th May 2021 (date of explosion). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Regulatory non-compliance and technical failure. Its causes are: Operation without a regulatory exemption and the coincidental explosion of the Callide C4 unit.

title: Low reserves projected post Eraring closure… and earlier?

The assessment projects a possible increase in unserved energy, particularly after August 2025, aligning with the Eraring closure, and highlights a potential reliability concern for the electricity market. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Structural and operational. Its causes are: Closure of the Eraring plant, potential unserved energy increase, anticipated low reserve conditions, increased loss of load probability post-closure.

title: Not so fast … the Bouldercombe Battery is not yet back into service, after the fire
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Not so fast … the Bouldercombe Battery is not yet back into service, after the fire" posted at 17-10-2023 01:53:00 PM is: The Bouldercombe Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Queensland, Australia, remains out of service following a fire incident that occurred on September 27, 2023. Although the battery briefly started charging again on September 28, its operational capacity has since returned to zero, as noted by the 'Bids & Offers' widget on the ez2view platform. The fire happened during a commissioning period, and no further updates have been released by Genex on the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) regarding this incident. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown, pending resolution of operational capacity issues. Its root_cause is: Unknown, as the cause of the fire has not been disclosed. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: September 27, 2023, with a brief operational period on September 28, 2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and potentially energy consumers depending on battery dispatch. Its cause type is: Operational failure during the commissioning period. Its causes are: Fire incident of unknown origin; post-fire operational failure.

title: Very Fast FCAS – a week in review
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very Fast FCAS – a week in review" posted on 13-10-2023 04:03:00 PM is: The article reviews the first week of the newly initiated Fast Frequency Response markets (1-second raise and 1-second lower) that began on October 9, 2023. It details the pricing trends observed, where LOWER1SEC prices consistently ranged from 0 to $140/MWh, often higher than the RAISE1SEC prices, which remained around $20/MWh. The cap on enablement levels is set at 50 MW. Discussions suggest that C&I aggregators primarily participate in RAISE1SEC offerings, leading to lower prices. Batteries have a reducing FCAS capacity at higher energy levels. Pricing is influenced by key players, with VENUS1 and EnelX’s ASNENC1 frequently setting RAISE1SEC prices, and various batteries influencing LOWER1SEC prices. Uncapped FCAS requirements could potentially exceed current caps, warranting attention to future changes in caps and registered capacities. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Market dynamics in pricing and participation strategies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: October 9, 2023, to October 13, 2023. Its affected region is: Australia (implied). Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders and participants. Its cause type is: Market behavior. Its causes are: Pricing inconsistencies between LOWER1SEC and RAISE1SEC, C&I aggregator strategy affecting RAISE1SEC prices, and participation patterns of battery storage systems.

title: The *other* market change that went live today (Monday 9th October 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The *other* market change that went live today (Monday 9th October 2023)" posted at 09-10-2023 12:18:00 PM is: The announcement highlights two market changes that took effect on October 9, 2023. The first is the implementation of the Very Fast Raise and Lower FCAS markets, driven by declining grid inertia due to coal unit closures. This change coincides with ongoing discussions at the AFR's Energy & Climate Summit. The second change is an enhancement to the MT PASA DUID Availability dataset, initiated by an ERM rule change, which now includes Unit State and Recall Time information but is limited to fully Scheduled units. These changes reflect ongoing adaptations in the energy market amid a transitioning energy landscape. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, with ongoing implications. Its root_cause is: Transition in energy market and reduction in grid inertia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-10-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Policy and market structure changes. Its causes are: Closure of coal-fired power units, energy market evolution, and the need for updated market data and tools.

title: Very Fast Raise and Lower FCAS markets to start on 9 October 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very Fast Raise and Lower FCAS markets to start on 9 October 2023" posted at 06-10-2023 03:39:00 PM is: The Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) is set to launch two new Contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets on October 9, 2023, focusing on "Very fast" Raise and Lower services designed to address frequency changes within a 1-second timeframe. These new markets, referred to as R1/L1 or 1-Second Raise/Lower, will supplement the existing FCAS services, which operate over longer timescales. The introduction of these markets aims to mitigate the risks posed by declining system inertia and corresponding increases in frequency deviation during contingency events, thus reducing the likelihood of under-frequency load shedding and potentially lowering costs associated with fast FCAS requirements. The launch includes participants like large batteries and comes with dynamic constraints to cap requirement levels at 50 MW. Predispatch market prices indicate significant pricing, with expectations of up to 300 $/MWh for 1-Second Lower and less than 200 $/MWh for 1-Second Raise services; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from October 9, 2023. Its root_cause is: Introduction of new FCAS markets to address declining system inertia and manage frequency deviations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-10-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: FCAS market participants, primarily those managing large battery systems. Its cause type is: Market and systemic change. Its causes are: Decline in system inertia, need for enhanced frequency response services, regulatory changes, and market readiness.

title: AER release ‘State of the Energy Market’ report for 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER release ‘State of the Energy Market’ report for 2023" posted at 05-10-2023 03:39:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released its annual 'State of the Energy Market' report, highlighting improved outcomes in wholesale energy markets with a significant decrease in wholesale electricity and gas prices compared to 2022. However, retail electricity prices rose by 9% to 20% across all NEM jurisdictions in 2022-23, exacerbating the cost-of-living pressures on households. Concerns were also raised about the increasing concentration of flexible generation assets and reduced market competition, especially in NSW and Victoria. AER Chair Clare Savage emphasized the increased market power of Snowy Hydro and noted limited liquidity in exchange-traded hedging products and a reduced number of clearing service providers. The AER anticipates new powers for better contract market monitoring to address these issues. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The impacts noted in the report refer to the 2022-23 period. Its root cause is: Improved outcomes in wholesale energy markets and concerns about market concentration affecting competition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-10-2023. Its affected region is: Primarily NSW and Victoria, but all NEM jurisdictions were discussed. Its affected key users are: Households in NEM jurisdictions. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and energy market competition. Its causes are: Significant decrease in wholesale electricity and gas prices, increased market concentration of flexible generation assets, reduced liquidity in hedging products, and limited competition as highlighted by the AER.

Commenters noted curtailment in some locales, although official sources such as AEMO and SA Power Networks have not reported coordinated curtailment measures. title: New lowest point for NEM-wide demand also set on Sunday 1st October 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New lowest point for NEM-wide demand also set on Sunday 1st October 2023" posted at 02-10-2023 12:48:00 PM is: On Sunday, October 1st, 2023, the National Electricity Market (NEM) observed new low points for demand across Queensland (QLD) and South Australia (SA), as well as NEM-wide. The 'Market Demand' dropped to 10,500MW at the 11:35 dispatch interval, marking a new low compared to previous records. The 'Operational Demand' also hit a new low at 11,224MW for the half-hour ending at 12:00 NEM time. These low demand levels are attributed to a lull ahead of the anticipated summer peaks. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) confirmed these records and continues to monitor the demand trends closely. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, with potential continued low demand until summer 2023-24. Its root_cause is: Low electricity demand levels in QLD and SA. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-10-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically Queensland and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants and electricity providers. Its cause type is: Demand fluctuations. Its causes are: Low market demand and operational demand levels in the NEM.

classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD minimum ‘Market Demand’ (in ordinary times) drops lower still, on Sunday 1st October 2023" posted at 01-10-2023 11:04:00 AM is: On the morning of October 1, 2023, Queensland's 'Market Demand' fell to an unprecedented low of 3,181 MW during ordinary times, observed at the 10:50 dispatch interval, surpassing the previous record set on September 17, 2023. The 'Operational Demand' further dropped to 3,131 MW for the half-hours ending at 11:00 and 11:30, marking a new low. Such drops in demand are noted as occurring under routine conditions, excluding anomalies like those from May 25, 2021, after a significant grid event. title: Another (Tesla) big battery fire in the NEM … this one at Bouldercombe BESS in QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another (Tesla) big battery fire in the NEM … this one at Bouldercombe BESS in QLD" posted at 27-09-2023 08:50:00 AM is: A fire broke out at the Bouldercombe Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at around 7:45 PM on 26th September 2023, located in Bouldercombe, Queensland. The fire started in a lithium battery storage unit and caused hazardous smoke in the vicinity. Emergency services, including the Queensland Police and Fire and Emergency Services, responded to the incident. Although the blaze was contained, crews continued to monitor the situation due to the risk of the fire spreading to other units at the site. The fire impacted the operation of the battery, which had been running prior to the incident, causing a dispatch error. A rebid was submitted to drop the unit's availability to 0 MW due to plant-related issues. The fire echoed a previous incident at the Victoria Big Battery in 2021 and raised concerns over energy supply reliability heading into the summer months. Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term, potentially affecting market operations and energy supply reliability for upcoming months, including summer; Its root cause is: Fire in a lithium storage unit; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 27-09-2023 and 26-09-2023; Its affected region is: Bouldercombe, Queensland; Its affected key users are: Unknown; Its cause type is: Technical failure; Its causes are: Fire in a lithium battery storage unit, hazardous smoke, dispatch error, plant-related issues leading to reduced availability.

title: Negative ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Saturday 23rd September 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Negative ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Saturday 23rd September 2023" posted at 23-09-2023 01:59:00 PM is: On 23rd September 2023, South Australia experienced an unprecedented negative 'Market Demand' of -14MW, noted during the 13:50 dispatch interval. This marked one of the lowest levels registered in recent times, following earlier occurrences in 2021. This dip is attributed particularly to adjustments in the operation modes of wind farms transitioning from non-scheduled to semi-scheduled units, a shift influenced by challenges associated with declining minimum demand. The previous record low 'Market Demand' for South Australia was observed on 16th September 2023 at -18MW. While 'Operational Demand' did not reach a new record, the region still noted a low of 23MW as compared to the record low of 21MW on 16th September 2023. Interesting dynamics in the electricity market are imminent, with changes to grid management and stability becoming significant discussion points. Additionally, there was no curtailment observed for rooftop PV despite concerns in October 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and likely short-term. Its root_cause is: Transition and re-basing of wind farm operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-09-2023, with references to earlier critical dates 21-11-2021 and 27-11-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but broadly includes grid operators and energy consumers in the region. Its cause type is: System operation adjustments. Its causes are: Transition of wind farms to semi-scheduled units, declining market demand in the electricity grid, and market-based dispatch mechanisms contributing to negative pricing moments.

title: Federal Court finding relating to Pelican Point CCGT on 8th February 2017 … and upcoming market changes
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Federal Court finding relating to Pelican Point CCGT on 8th February 2017 … and upcoming market changes" posted at 21-09-2023 01:32:00 PM is: The article discusses a Federal Court judgement related to the 2017 load shedding event in South Australia involving Pelican Point Power. The judgement, favorable to the Australian Energy Regulator (AER), emphasizes the importance of accurate information from power generators to the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to maintain system security. The article also highlights upcoming changes effective from October 9, 2023, including the introduction of new commodities in the Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets and enhanced data requirements for power generators. Moreover, it discusses the potential challenges for the electricity market in the upcoming summer due to predicted hotter and drier conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Industrial Action at Kogan Creek Power station part 2 … it starts today, and here’s where to watch live output
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Industrial Action at Kogan Creek Power station part 2 … it starts today, and here’s where to watch live output" posted at 21-09-2023 09:38:00 AM is: The Kogan Creek Power Station is facing industrial action starting Thursday, led by the Mining and Energy Union workers. This action threatens 10% of Queensland's power generation capacity, as workers plan to initially halt coal conveyor belts and later reduce the plant's megawatt output during peak periods, potentially affecting power supply. Meanwhile, a live updating dashboard is available to monitor the station's output and the QLD region's reserve margins to gauge supply-demand tightness. CS Energy has released statements regarding the negotiations and planned industrial action. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its cause type is: Labor dispute. Its causes are: Planned industrial action, shutdown of coal conveyor belts, reduction in power plant output during peak periods.

title: BOM declares that El Niño and a positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) are underway
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "BOM declares that El Niño and a positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) are underway" posted at 19-09-2023 04:16:00 PM is: The Bureau of Meteorology (BOM) has announced that both El Niño and a positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) are underway, which is expected to result in warmer and drier conditions across southern and eastern Australia from October to December. This climatic shift is causing concerns about tight supply-demand balance and potential shortfalls in electricity supply for the summer of 2023-24, as forecasted by AEMO. This situation is significant since it's the first El Niño since 2015/16 and the first positive IOD since 2019; its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: October to December 2023, and potentially more extended impacts into summer 2023-24. Its root cause is: Climatic patterns involving El Niño and a positive Indian Ocean Dipole. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-09-2023, with climatic patterns expected from October 2023 onwards. Its affected region is: Southern and eastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Climatic event. Its causes are: El Niño and positive Indian Ocean Dipole declared by BOM, leading to potential electricity market impacts.

title: About the low Operational Demand on Sat 16th Sept and Sun 17th Sept 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About the low Operational Demand on Sat 16th Sept and Sun 17th Sept 2023" posted at 19-09-2023 02:07:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant decline in operational demand in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on the weekend of September 16th and 17th, 2023. Notably, the demand reached record lows due to a combination of high solar generation and moderate underlying demand, causing significant curtailment of renewable energy sources. The incident also saw impressively high levels of Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM). South Australia, in particular, set a new operational minimum demand record, and there was a substantial impact on the electricity market with negative pricing and elevated Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) prices during the period. There was no mention of specific disruptions or failures, but the occurrence raises questions about the orchestration of distributed energy resources and future grid management strategies. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, pertaining to the weekend of September 16th and 17th, 2023. Its root cause is: High levels of solar generation coupled with moderate demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-09-2023 and 17-09-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), with particular reference to South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Grid/market phenomenon. Its causes are: Record low operational demand, high renewable energy generation, negative energy pricing, high levels of electric grid IRPM.

title: QLD’s turn at new lowest point for minimum demand (in ordinary times)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD’s turn at new lowest point for minimum demand" posted at 17-09-2023 11:31:00 AM is: On September 17, 2023, Queensland (QLD) experienced a new low in electricity demand, recording a market demand of 3,201MW at the 10:25 dispatch interval, lower than the previous record of 3,250MW set on August 20, 2023. This reduced demand is part of a trend observed across other regions like Victoria (VIC), South Australia (SA), and the National Electricity Market (NEM), excluding anomalies caused by events like the Callide C4 Catastrophe. The demand may continue to decrease into the spring season.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction/residential surveys.
