title: New ‘lowest ever*’ points for Market Demand–in Victoria and NEM-wide on Saturday 16th September 2023 (and record high IRPM)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New ‘lowest ever*’ points for Market Demand–in Victoria and NEM-wide on Saturday 16th September 2023 (and record high IRPM)" posted at 16-09-2023 12:14:00 PM is: On September 16th, 2023, the National Electricity Market (NEM) and Victoria experienced record-low market demand due to seasonal factors, specifically spring weather, leading to unusual electricity consumption patterns. Victoria's market demand fell to an unprecedented low of 1,735MW, while the NEM-wide demand dropped to 10,733MW, setting new historical lows. At the same time, the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) reached a record high of 264.02%, attributed to high Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) output. These occurrences underline the significant shifts in energy demand and generation profiles within the market, as evidenced by low demand levels across several mainland NEM regions, including South Australia. These dynamics were characterized in part by the high contribution of rooftop photovoltaic systems and the curtailment of large solar and wind resources.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market Reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially influencing future energy market dynamics. Root_cause is: Seasonal spring weather patterns leading to low electricity demand and high renewable energy generation. Accident scale is: N/A. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 16-09-2023. Affected region is: Victoria, and broader NEM, including South Australia. Affected key users are: Electricity market operators and participants. Cause type is: Weather-induced and renewable energy integration. Causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Seasonal spring weather, increased VRE output, operational changes in energy generation (e.g., rooftop PV, wind and solar curtailment).

title: Extended long-term trend of wind production statistics (to the end of August 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Extended long-term trend of wind production statistics (to the end of August 2023)" posted at 15-09-2023 02:20:00 PM is: The article discusses the long-term trends in wind farm capacity factors across the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting that the optimistic capacity factors assumed by some reports, such as those by CSIRO, may not align with actual performance. The capacity factors influence metrics like the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE), with higher factors leading to lower calculated costs. The article also notes variations in capacity factors over time, citing economic and technical constraints such as commercial curtailment and transmission issues that affect actual performance. It suggests that while wind energy remains crucial, optimism regarding capacity factors should be tempered based on historical data. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, spanning from January 2020 to August 2023. Its root_cause is: Misalignment between projected and actual capacity factors due to curtailment and other constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-09-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market performance and policy assumptions. Its causes are: Capacity factor assumptions, economic curtailment, and technical constraints.

title: AEMC proposes to progressively increase MPC to $22,800/MWh by 1 July 2027 (amongst other changes)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC proposes to progressively increase MPC to $22,800/MWh by 1 July 2027 (amongst other changes)" posted at 14-09-2023 11:45:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has proposed a draft rule change to progressively increase the Market Price Cap (MPC) in the wholesale electricity market from the current $16,600/MWh to $22,800/MWh by 1 July 2027. Additionally, the Cumulative Price Threshold, which indicates sustained periods of extreme high prices, will be extended from 7.5 hours to 8.5 hours at the MPC, and the Administered Price Cap will be set at $600/MWh from 1 July 2025. This draft rule change is open for submissions until 26 October 2023. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1 July 2025 to 1 July 2027 as per the proposed timeline for implementation. Its root_cause is: A proposed regulatory change to manage price ceilings in the wholesale electricity market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-09-2023 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: Potentially all regions within the Australian electricity market. Its affected key users are: Market participants, including electricity producers and consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory proposal. Its causes are: Proposed increases to the Market Price Cap, Cumulative Price Threshold, and Administered Price Cap.

title: System Strength constraints strike again in northern QLD – this time on Wednesday 6th September 2023 (Part 1 of Case Study?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "System Strength constraints strike again in northern QLD – this time on Wednesday 6th September 2023 (Part 1 of Case Study?)" posted at 11-09-2023 03:02:00 PM is: On Wednesday, 6th September 2023, many Semi-Scheduled (Wind and Solar) units in northern Queensland experienced simultaneous curtailment just before midday due to system strength constraints. Several constraint equations, especially within the 'Q-BCNE_821' and 'Q-CPWU_818' sets, were bound during this period due to outages on key transmission lines. Notably, the binding constraints significantly reduced the output from these units, impacting electricity prices. The outages began on Monday, 4th September 2023, and some are scheduled to end on 11th October 2023. The curtailment reoccurrence on this date draws parallels to similar incidents, such as the one on 13th October 2020. Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 6th September 2023 through 11th October 2023. Its root_cause is: system strength constraints and network outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 6th September 2023. Its affected region is: Northern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled Wind and Solar units. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational. Its causes are: System strength constraints related to outages on the 'line 821 between Bouldercombe and Nebo' and 'line 818 between Calliope River and Wurdong'.

title: Long or short on storage? A look into price signals for duration
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Long or short on storage? A look into price signals for duration" posted at 07-09-2023 04:40:00 AM is: The article discusses the emerging need for utility-scale energy storage in Queensland's energy market, highlighting varying opinions on the necessary duration to support a grid heavily reliant on variable renewable energy (VRE). It compares models proposing different amounts of storage duration, from only a few hours to multiple instances of large projects like Snowy 2.0. The current market favors short-duration storage projects, finding them attractive for arbitrage options due to increased price volatility. Long-duration storage faces challenges due to its uncertain return on additional investments. The analysis uses thought experiments to simulate revenue from different storage durations, ultimately concluding that the current market conditions make longer-duration projects financially unjustifiable without external incentives or changes to market arrangements. Solar and wind power droughts are identified as high-impact, low-probability events that markets find difficult to price accurately. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: The debate on the duration of energy storage needed for a VRE-dependent grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-09-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and potentially the broader National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Examination of market dynamics related to energy storage investment. Its causes are: Varied analyses and simulations on storage duration needed for effective grid management, emerging arbitrage opportunities from price volatility, and lack of current market incentives for long-duration storage investments.

title: An ESOO snippet – how effective are different technologies at enhancing reliability?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "An ESOO snippet – how effective are different technologies at enhancing reliability?" posted at 06-09-2023 03:56:00 PM is: The article discusses the insights from AEMO's 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) focused on the types and amounts of new energy capacities needed to close emerging reliability gaps as ageing thermal stations retire. The analysis reveals that open-cycle gas turbines and storage with longer durations (six to eight hours) are highly effective substitutes for "fully-firm" capacity, contributing significantly to reliability. In contrast, non-dispatchable renewable sources, such as wind and solar, show much lower effectiveness in closing reliability gaps due to their variability, with effectiveness varying between New South Wales and Victoria due to differences in existing renewable capacity and weather conditions. The piece emphasizes not extrapolating these findings beyond the specific regional and temporal contexts modeled in the ESOO. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid-to-late 2020s. Its root_cause is: Retirement of ageing thermal stations without sufficient new capacity commitments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-09-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Structural/Market. Its causes are: Retirement of ageing thermal stations, differences in renewable capacity effects between states, and insufficient new energy capacity commitments.

title: NSW Government to explore option for (relatively short) extension to operations at Eraring Power Station … amongst other initiatives
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW Government to explore option for (relatively short) extension to operations at Eraring Power Station … amongst other initiatives" posted at 06-09-2023 09:59:00 AM is: The NSW Government is considering extending the operations at the Eraring Power Station amidst challenges of transitioning to renewable energy. This move responds to recommendations outlined in the Marsden Jacob report, emphasizing the need for more time to build necessary infrastructure as the potential 2025 closure of Eraring looms. The government recognizes the role of Eraring in maintaining electricity supply and reliability in NSW and plans to consult with Origin Energy, which is also interested in deferring the closure. The developments indicate a need to balance electricity supply with renewable energy targets as the region navigates an energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 3. The causes are: Potential early closure of Eraring, need for additional renewable energy infrastructure, policy adjustments for smoother transition.

title: Stock up on candles?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stock up on candles?" posted at 06-09-2023 09:14:00 AM is: The article discusses potential electricity supply issues in Victoria, attributed primarily to the ageing infrastructure and anticipated high summer demands. It highlights a relatively low risk of supply shortfalls, calculated at 0.0008%-0.0009%, compared to disruptions caused by local network issues, such as the example of AEMO’s bubble chart predictions, showcasing various reliability scenarios. The majority of the assessed scenarios suggest no supply shortfalls, though in cases of extreme heat, a few scenarios predict supply gaps reaching up to 500 MW. AEMO's contingency plans include short-term solutions like RERT to prevent prolonged outages for individuals. The overall message stresses the importance of addressing the medium and long-term risks posed by retiring power stations and delays in new infrastructure projects. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2023/24. Its root_cause is: Ageing generators and potential generator breakdowns amidst high demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-09-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Victorian electricity customers. Its cause type is: Technological and environmental. Its causes are: Ageing power stations, potential increased summer demand, and insufficient coordination in new supply infrastructure.

title: It only took a day (after release of the 2023 ESOO) to get to RERT Tenders for summer 2023-24
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It only took a day (after release of the 2023 ESOO) to get to RERT Tenders for summer 2023-24" posted at 01-09-2023 02:13:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) quickly moved to call tenders for Interim Reliability Reserves in South Australia and Victoria just a day after releasing the 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO). This decision follows the forecast that these regions are likely to exceed the stipulated Interim Reliability Criteria by the summer of 2023-24, indicating a need for out-of-market responses due to potential market failure. The situation is seen as a reflection of the industry's challenges in meeting electricity demand without resorting to reserve measures and has sparked discussions on prolonging the operation of ageing thermal power stations. title: Here’s 7 Observations that jumped out to me in a first skim of the 2023 ESOO
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Here’s 7 Observations that jumped out to me in a first skim of the 2023 ESOO" posted at 31-08-2023 03:32:00 PM is: The 2023 update to the Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) by AEMO presents several critical observations about the state of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Key points include concerns over the effectiveness and clarity of the ESOO's purposes, the dire projections concerning unserved energy, and the lack of a clear contingency plan for anticipated future challenges, especially regarding the reliability of coal and gas units. The report's modelling has been enhanced, though it's not free from the inherent limitations of forecasts. Additionally, there are concerns on the demand-side challenges and the need for a robust response mechanism. Moments of high demand and inadequate supply might lead to potential blackouts unless significant intervention or unforeseen positive developments occur. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to long-term implications. Its root_cause is: Inadequate supply commitment and grid reliability issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers across the NEM. Its cause type is: Projected inadequacies in energy supply and grid reliability. Its causes are: Incomplete supply commitments, reliability challenges with coal and gas units, and operational limitations outlined in AEMO's projections.

title: With the 2023 ESOO report, some real head scratching about Demand Response!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With the 2023 ESOO report, some real head scratching about Demand Response!" posted at 31-08-2023 03:16:00 PM is: The article highlights concerns and evaluations related to Demand Response (DR) within the 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) for Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM). Despite initiatives like the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism (WDRM), participation remains low, with only 67 MW registered, slightly more than the previous year's 66 MW. In contrast, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) projects a sizeable expansion of DR, particularly in New South Wales, anticipating an increase from 337 MW to 1,200 MW over a decade, driven by the NSW Government’s Peak Demand Reduction Scheme. The report underscores the imperative for further analysis of DR's credibility and potential contributions.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term projection (next 10 years). Its root_cause is: Underwhelming participation in Demand Response initiatives. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-08-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market in Australia, especially New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Large energy users and DR program participants. Its cause type is: Policy and market mechanism evaluation. Its causes is: Low uptake in WDRM, AEMO's high DR projections, NSW Government’s Peak Demand Reduction Scheme growth forecast.

title: With the 2023 ESOO report, different views about which USE figure should the AEMO have led with?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With the 2023 ESOO report, different views about which USE figure should the AEMO have led with?" posted at 31-08-2023 01:32:00 PM is: The article discusses differing opinions regarding the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) 2023 ESOO report, specifically addressing which figure they should have emphasized for unserved energy (USE). The AEMO's use of a "central scenario" highlighting potential blackouts and energy supply issues has drawn criticism from some commentators who suggest that a "sensitivity scenario" showing potential benefits of various projects and consumer investments should have been highlighted. Despite debates, the report's projection of energy supply challenges aligns with concerns previously raised by journalists; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).
