title: The 2023 ESOO report shows coal unit EFOR is still a significant factor
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The 2023 ESOO report shows coal unit EFOR is still a significant factor," posted on 31-08-2023 01:14:00 PM is: The article discusses the findings of the 2023 ESOO report by AEMO, which highlights the ongoing and increasing challenge of the equivalent forced outage rate (EFOR) for coal units as a critical factor. The report notes that the EFOR is higher than historical levels due to operational challenges and the energy transition, and it is expected to worsen as coal units age. The closure of the Liddell units is expected to reduce the average EFOR for NSW coal units. However, there are concerns about how unexpected EFOR trends may impact unserved energy (USE) projections. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing and worsening as coal units age. Its root_cause is: aging coal units and operational challenges. Its accident scale is: unknown. Its dates are: the analysis includes data up to 31st December 2022. Its affected region is: primarily New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: electricity market stakeholders in the region. Its cause type is: operational and aging infrastructure issues. Its causes are: higher EFOR rates in coal units, aging infrastructure, and closure of poor availability units like Liddell.

title: The 2023 ESOO report clarifies why the transmission model only includes ‘System Normal’ – and why it’s a simplification
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The 2023 ESOO report clarifies why the transmission model only includes ‘System Normal’ – and why it’s a simplification" posted at 31-08-2023 12:40:00 PM is: The news discusses the 2023 ESOO report's explanation of its transmission model, which only considers 'System Normal' conditions while excluding outage constraints. This simplification, highlighted by AEMO, may overlook significant factors affecting market outcomes and reliability, which include risks from abnormal transmission conditions. The report aims to clarify the modeling approach, but concerns about the exclusion of potentially impactful constraints remain significant. The constraints not only affect market outcomes in terms of financial impacts but also have implications for system reliability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing, as the exclusion of factors can have prolonged impacts. Its root cause is: Simplification in the 2023 ESOO model by excluding certain constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-08-2023 12:40:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market operators. Its cause type is: Modelling and data exclusion. Its causes is: Exclusion of outage constraints in the 2023 ESOO transmission model, reliance on 'System Normal' conditions.

title: The 2023 ESOO modelling provides a more accurate real world depiction of Wind Farm limitations at high temperatures
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The 2023 ESOO modelling provides a more accurate real-world depiction of Wind Farm limitations at high temperatures" posted at 31-08-2023 11:48:00 AM is: The article discusses how the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has enhanced its 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) modeling to better account for the known limitation of wind farm output during high temperatures. While these conditions have existed previously, the improved modeling now accurately reflects reality, mitigating misconceptions about increased risk. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: medium-term as it involves improved modeling practices. Its root cause is: limitations of wind farm output at high temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: electricity market participants and planners. Its cause type is: operational inefficiency due to environmental factors. Its causes are: high temperature cutout and calm but hot weather conditions affecting wind farm performance.

title: AEMO releases 2023 ESOO on Thursday 31st August 2023 (Australia’s NEM is perched on the edge)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases 2023 ESOO on Thursday 31st August 2023 (Australia’s NEM is perched on the edge)" posted at 31-08-2023 08:03:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released its 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO), highlighting urgent investment needs in Australia's energy market to ensure long-term reliability and affordability. The report predicts significant reliability gaps, primarily due to the anticipated retirement of 62% of the current coal fleet by 2033, which may lead to potential power shortages. AEMO emphasizes the need for immediate and substantial investment in energy infrastructure to manage the transition to cleaner energy sources. The report sparked extensive media coverage, signaling broader concerns about energy stability, particularly with predicted blackouts in the looming El Niño summer. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Over the next 10 years. Its root_cause is: The anticipated retirement of a significant portion of the coal-fired power fleet. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2023. Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM) areas, particularly eastern regions. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers and the energy sector. Its cause type is: Policy and infrastructural transformation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Anticipated coal fleet retirements, delayed investments in new energy infrastructure, and projected El Niño weather events leading to increased risks of power shortages.

title: Power Surge on 30th August 2023 affects Azure Services – and hence AEMO services (and GR services)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Power Surge on 30th August 2023 affects Azure Services – and hence AEMO services (and GR services)" posted at 31-08-2023 06:36:00 AM is: A power surge on 30th August 2023 led to significant challenges for Microsoft Azure services in the Australia East region, which also impacted the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) applications and Market Data services. This incident, possibly triggered by storms in Sydney, resulted in a utility power surge that deactivated cooling units and led to increased temperatures, necessitating the proactive shutdown of certain computing and storage units to prevent hardware damage. While Microsoft has been working diligently to restore services, with significant progress made, issues remain for some virtual machines and storage services as of the latest update. AEMO declared a major incident due to the disruption, affecting various critical services. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term until services are fully restored. Its root_cause is: A utility power surge affecting Azure's datacenter operations. Its accident scale is: Regional impact in Australia East and Southeast. Its dates are: 30th August 2023. Its affected region is: Australia East and Southeast. Its affected key users are: Microsoft Azure users, AEMO, and clients dependent on these services. Its cause type is: External environmental factors. Its causes are: Power surge likely caused by storms, leading to cooling and power shutdown in Azure's data center.

title: Case Study of high AggSchedTarget on Thu 30th Jan 2020 and Fri 31st Jan 2020 (part 1!)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of high AggSchedTarget on Thu 30th Jan 2020 and Fri 31st Jan 2020 (part 1!)" posted at 30-08-2023 03:58:00 PM is: The article provides a detailed analysis of the extreme levels of Aggregate Scheduled Target (AggSchedTarget) in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) on January 30 and 31, 2020. Despite significant investments in renewable energy sources like wind, solar, and rooftop PV, the case study highlights the persistent high and unpredictable demand for firming capacity, which exceeded previous records on these two days. The peak values notably surpassed other high-demand days, emphasizing the necessity for adequate backup systems to handle such extreme events. The analysis of data from these dates sheds light on the challenges posed by energy transitions, particularly in ensuring grid stability during demand spikes. The event foreshadows upcoming discussions around energy policies and the 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities report. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate effects on January 30-31, 2020, with broader implications for future grid planning. Its root cause is: High demand for firming capacity driven by extreme weather and insufficiently predictable renewable output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: January 30-31, 2020. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Utilities and consumers relying on electricity supply from the NEM. Its cause type is: Extreme demand and insufficient firming capacity. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High electricity demand due to extreme weather conditions and insufficient backup capacity to cover the demands, revealing grid challenges.

title: 2+ hour bout of spot price volatility in SA on Wednesday morning 30th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "2+ hour bout of spot price volatility in SA on Wednesday morning 30th August 2023" posted at 30-08-2023 09:23:00 AM is: The South Australian electricity market experienced significant spot price volatility on the morning of August 30th, 2023, with prices soaring above $1,000/MWh, peaking at $12,600.44/MWh during a two-hour period starting at 06:05 and ending with the last spike at 08:25. This marked a challenging period for stakeholders monitoring the region through tools like NEMwatch. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Sudden changes in electricity demand or supply dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-08-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Market-driven. Its causes are: Unexpected spikes in electricity demand and market price escalation in South Australia.

title: EnergyAustralia notes the likely change of operating mode for Mt Piper coal-fired power station in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "EnergyAustralia notes the likely change of operating mode for Mt Piper coal-fired power station in NSW" posted at 27-08-2023 06:11:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia has announced plans to transition the Mt Piper coal-fired power station in New South Wales into a back-up role from its current baseload operation well ahead of its planned closure in 2040. Due to increased variability in energy supply as solar and wind displace fossil fuel sources, Mt Piper is set to provide grid stability and renewable energy firming services. While shifting towards reduced output and more flexible operations, the move responds to uncertainties in delivering sufficient renewable and firming capacities within the grid. This strategy reflects the broader challenges in balancing supply and demand amid the transition away from coal-fired and gas-fired power stations. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to Long Term. Its root_cause is: Transition from coal to renewable energy and grid stability needs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-08-2023 (meeting publication), 2040 (targeted closure date of Mt Piper). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Policy/Operational Changes. Its causes are: Transition in energy operations from baseload to firming and reserve roles for Mt Piper; Uncertainty in delivering transmission, renewable, and firming projects timely; Variability in energy supply due to the rise in solar and wind usage.

title: Reports of recommendations to consider extending the life of Eraring Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reports of recommendations to consider extending the life of Eraring Power Station" posted at 27-08-2023 06:01:00 PM is: The Eraring Power Station, Australia's largest coal-fired power plant in NSW, may continue operations beyond its initially planned 2025 shutdown. This consideration follows a government review suggesting an extension to ensure electricity reliability during NSW's transition to renewable energy, as replacement projects face delays. A confidential cabinet document reportedly recommended negotiating with Origin Energy to temporarily prolong the plant's life to prevent energy shortfalls, potentially costing $200 to $400 million annually. The decision awaits a final verdict as the government reviews the confidential report delivered earlier this month.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Unknown. Root_cause: Delay in renewable energy projects. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-08-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy consideration. Its causes are: Potential extension of Eraring Power Station's operation beyond 2025 to ensure reliability during energy transition.

title: AGL Energy and VIC Govt entered into ‘Structured Transition Agreement’ relating to Loy Yang A
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AGL Energy and VIC Govt entered into ‘Structured Transition Agreement’ relating to Loy Yang A" posted at 27-08-2023 05:54:00 PM is: AGL Energy and the Victorian Government have established a Structured Transition Agreement to coordinate the operation, maintenance, and planned closure of the Loy Yang A Power Station by 30 June 2035. This agreement includes a risk-sharing mechanism to prevent the plant’s premature shutdown due to market conditions, ensuring system reliability. The deal reflects ongoing governmental measures to transition from coal-dependent energy infrastructure while maintaining energy stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy . The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, lasting until the scheduled closure date in 2035. Its root_cause is: Transitioning energy policy towards reduced dependency on coal. Its accident scale is: Unknown, as it is based on policy transition rather than an accidental event. Its dates are: 21st August 2023, for when the announcement was made. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Victorian electricity consumers and AGL Energy. Its cause type is: Policy-driven. Its causes are: Structured transition agreement and planned government intervention to manage Loy Yang A’s operation and closure.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q2 2023 released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q2 2023 released" posted at 24-08-2023 03:40:00 PM is: The article announces the release of the GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q2 2023, a comprehensive analysis collaboration between Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting. Released on 18th August 2023, this update is part of a series that explores various aspects of the energy transition. The report includes an executive summary and six appendices covering metrics like system inertia, grid demand patterns, wind and solar performance, dispatchable capacity, coal unit dependability, and network dynamics. Each section offers detailed analyses on historical data, demand forecasting, and renewable energy integration, among others. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing/Quarterly. Its root cause is: Regular publication series analyzing market trends and energy transitions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18th August 2023 (release date). Its affected region is: Unknown (assumed as pertaining broadly to regions in the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Policymakers, energy analysts, and market participants. Its cause type is: Analytical and informative report release. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Continued monitoring and reporting of energy transition metrics and system performance.

title: New low point for ‘Market Demand’ in QLD on Sunday 20th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New low point for ‘Market Demand’ in QLD on Sunday 20th August 2023" posted at 20-08-2023 11:58:00 AM is: The article discusses a new record low for market demand in Queensland, Australia, on a sunny and warm winter Sunday. Market demand dropped to 3,281MW at 11:35 AM, below last year's low of 3,311MW. The reduction does not account for the operational demand, which had its lowest point at 3,460MW on the same day. This decline is partly due to seasonal weather conditions that reduced electricity usage. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, primarily affecting the specific day mentioned. Its root_cause is: Unseasonal warm weather reducing electricity demand. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 20-08-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand reduction. Its causes are: Warm weather, reduced usage rates.

title: Semi-Scheduled Bid MaxAvail – Initial Results
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Semi-Scheduled Bid MaxAvail – Initial Results" posted at 16-08-2023 03:38:00 PM is: The article discusses the recent implementation of a change allowing NEM Participants to use the energy bid's MaxAvail as a cap on semi-scheduled unit availability, starting from 7th August 2023. This new functionality aids in market transparency and offers a direct method for participants to manage availability for commercial reasons, such as avoiding negative prices. Within the first week, the change was observed in 357 intervals, mainly through rebids, affecting 24 units (6 solar, 18 wind) across five NEM regions, with usage peaking between 8 AM and 4 PM. The article provides specific examples, highlighting different motivations for restricting availability. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, starting 7th August 2023 through the subsequent week noted in the analysis. Its root_cause is: The introduction of the MaxAvail functionality for semi-scheduled units in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Implementation date on 7th August 2023, with data observed over the following week. Its affected region is: Various NEM regions including NSW, QLD, SA, TAS, and VIC. Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled generators and NEM market participants. Its cause type is: Policy implementation. Its causes are: Introduction of MaxAvail functionality, response to manage commercial challenges such as negative pricing, need for transparent market adjustments.

title: Evening volatility in SA, VIC, NSW and QLD on Tuesday 15th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in SA, VIC, NSW and QLD on Tuesday 15th August 2023" posted at 16-08-2023 10:39:00 AM is: The news reports on a significant price volatility event in the National Electricity Market (NEM) across the mainland regions of South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, and Queensland, occurring between 17:50 and 18:40 on 15th August 2023. Prices spiked above $1,000/MWh, with NSW reaching the Market Price Cap during this period, driven by winter-style evening peak demand and a lull in variable renewable energy (VRE) sources, including low wind (~600MW). The NEM-wide Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) fell to 18%, indicating a tighter supply-demand balance. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market Reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the evening of 15th August 2023. Its root_cause is: Combination of high demand and low renewable generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15th August 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market Conditions. Its causes are: High evening demand, low wind generation, solar unavailability, NEM-wide IRPM drop.

title: Price volatility in SA on Sunday afternoon 13th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in SA on Sunday afternoon 13th August 2023" posted at 13-08-2023 02:49:00 PM is: The electricity market in South Australia experienced price volatility on Sunday 13th August 2023, primarily due to a persistent network outage since Friday evening, reduced wind conditions, and increased cloud cover, leading to elevated electricity prices reaching up to $1,000/MWh. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: August 2023. Its root_cause is: Network outage combined with weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates is: 13-08-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Combined factors of network outage and weather conditions. Its causes is: Persistent network outage, reduced wind energy generation, increased cloud cover leading to decreased solar generation.

title: Over four hours of volatility in the SA region on Friday evening 11th August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Over four hours of volatility in the SA region on Friday evening 11th August 2023" posted at 11-08-2023 05:38:00 PM is: The article reports on significant spot price volatility in South Australia's electricity market, largely driven by low wind conditions, fading solar output, and the tripping of all units at the Snapper Point power station, which began at 20:35 on 11th August 2023. Prices reached highs of $14,391.35/MWh and continued fluctuating above $1,000/MWh till 22:05, and started again early the next morning. Contributing factors include constrained network limits and counter-price flows to Victoria. Additionally, forecast LOR (Lack of Reserve) conditions were noted, adding to the volatility concerns. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, with implications potentially extending due to forecasted LOR conditions. Its root_cause is: Low renewable energy output and generation unit trips. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11th August 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generation and network constraints. Its causes are: Low wind and solar output, Snapper Point generator trip, network constraint-induced counter-price flows, and LOR condition forecasts.

title: What inputs and processes determine a semi-scheduled unit’s availability
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "What inputs and processes determine a semi-scheduled unit’s availability" posted at 11-08-2023 03:51:00 PM is: This article explains the processes and inputs that determine the availability of a semi-scheduled generating unit, which is crucial for balancing electricity supply and demand in the dispatch timeframe. Key factors include the Unconstrained Intermittent Generation Forecast (UIGF), site-specific measurements such as wind speeds and solar irradiance, and market inputs like the MaxAvail value from energy bids. The UIGF is adopted unless capped by a lower MaxAvail bid, allowing for accurate dispatch and grid management. Changes introduced on August 7, 2023, emphasized the importance of commercial availability. The article also discusses the role of AEMO and participant self-forecasts in determining UIGF.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term, particularly impacting the immediate dispatch intervals. Its root_cause is: Changes in the process of determining the availability of semi-scheduled units for dispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-08-2023. Its affected region is: Primarily regions covered by the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants utilizing semi-scheduled units. Its cause type is: Process and regulatory update. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Introduction of a cap on UIGF using energy bid MaxAvail, update in AEMO forecasts and participant self-forecast usage, emphasis on site-based measurements and weather forecasts in setting UIGF.

title: Discussion Paper released on Capacity Investment Scheme
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Discussion Paper released on Capacity Investment Scheme" posted at 08-08-2023 12:17:00 PM is: The Australian federal Department of Energy released a Consultation Paper on 4th August 2023, detailing a proposed Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) intending to attract $10 billion in new investments and deliver 6 GW of clean dispatchable capacity by 2030. The proposal involves competitive tenders from 2023, supporting renewable and storage projects to improve energy reliability with long-term financial agreements. The paper emphasizes the role of the scheme in filling reliability gaps, with specific plans for partnerships and tenders in South Australia and Victoria. Key consultation dates include submissions due by 31st August 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Government's initiative to bolster energy reliability through investment in renewable and storage projects. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 8th December 2022 (eligibility for financial close), 4th August 2023 (release of Consultation Paper), 31st August 2023 (submission due date). Its affected region is: National focus, with specific mentions of partnerships in New South Wales, and tenders in South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy developers and proponents of clean renewable generation and storage projects. Its cause type is: Policy initiative. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Historical iterations to increase dispatchable energy capacity, such as the National Energy Guarantee (NEG), culminating in the current Capacity Investment Scheme proposal.

title: 7th Aug 2023 – EnergyAustralia ‘continues to focus on strengthening performance following improved first-half results’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "7th Aug 2023 – EnergyAustralia ‘continues to focus on strengthening performance following improved first-half results’" posted at 07-08-2023 10:00:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia has focused on enhancing performance following improved first-half results, highlighting improved operational reliability at Yallourn and a new agreement with Centennial to bolster coal reliability at Mt Piper. The company has invested $400 million to expedite maintenance at Yallourn, preparing for major outages ahead of the station's planned 2028 closure. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Mid to long-term until the planned closure in 2028. Its root cause is: Operational and strategic adjustments in response to past performance challenges and market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-08-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Strategic operational and maintenance decisions. Its causes are: Improved coal reliability, maintenance investments, and enhanced operational reliability.

title: Implemented: Enablement of bid energy MaxAvail for semi-scheduled generators
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Implemented: Enablement of bid energy MaxAvail for semi-scheduled generators" posted at 07-08-2023 05:09:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has implemented a change to the National Electricity Market Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) that supports the MaxAvail field in energy bids for semi-scheduled generators. Announced on August 7, 2023, this change allows semi-scheduled generators to cap their dispatch availability at a level lower than their unconstrained intermittent generation forecast (UIGF). Initially planned for mid-June, the implementation was delayed due to testing and risk management issues, eventually being executed on August 7. This change, now operational, reflects in NEMDE and PASA processes, allowing for better alignment between forecasted and actual available energy. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Implementation of new bidding process for semi-scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 07-08-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes is: Delays due to testing, scheduling dependencies, and risk management.

title: The proposed voluntary ‘Schedule Lite’ mechanism starts being deliberated at the AEMC
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The proposed voluntary ‘Schedule Lite’ mechanism starts being deliberated at the AEMC," posted at 03-08-2023 07:20:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has begun deliberations on the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) proposal for a 'Scheduled Lite' mechanism. This mechanism aims to integrate price-responsive distributed resources into market scheduling with a voluntary and flexible framework. The intention is to innovate and enhance competition in consumer services, providing visibility and dispatchability to address complex challenges, thus lowering costs. Consumer benefits include new revenue streams beyond existing tariffs. The proposal seeks optimal allocation of resources, maintaining security and reliability in Australia's energy system. Submissions for this proposal close on 14th September 2023.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, as the mechanism is intended to provide gradual integration and benefits. Its root cause is: The need to integrate price-responsive distributed resources to enhance system efficiency and reduce costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-08-2023 to 14-09-2023 (closure of submissions). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Consumers and market operators. Its cause type is: Proposal for market mechanism change. Its causes are: The proposal for integrating price-responsive resources to improve market scheduling and reduce reliance on interventions.

title: The VEPC paper ‘No longer lost in transmission’, and the AEMO response
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The VEPC paper ‘No longer lost in transmission’, and the AEMO response" posted at 02-08-2023 03:00:00 PM is: The article discusses the release of a report titled ‘No longer lost in transmission’ by VEPC, which presents an alternative plan ("Plan B") to the VNI-West development pursued by AEMO. The report addresses issues such as loss factors and curtailments affecting wind and solar power, proposing a different approach to transmission development. AEMO responded with their media release on the same day, indicating the report was reviewed prior to its publish date. The response highlights the ongoing debate concerning infrastructure planning and renewable integration in Victoria. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as it discusses future infrastructure planning. Its root_cause is: Disagreement over infrastructure planning and integration of renewables. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-08-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy policymakers and stakeholders in the renewable sector. Its cause type is: Debate on infrastructure planning. Its causes are: Dispute over VNI-West development, alternative planning proposals for renewables integration.

title: Looking back at FCAS on 1 August 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking back at FCAS on 1 August 2023" posted at 02-08-2023 02:33:00 PM is: The article discusses the spike in Fast Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) prices on the mainland for the 18:05 dispatch interval on 1 August 2023. The increased prices were a result of constrained Heywood interconnector, decreased wind generation across multiple NEM regions, and coincided with the evening peak demand, leading to elevated energy and FCAS prices from 18:00 to 19:00. Smaller batteries were noted to rebid due to "Updated SOC close to limit", highlighting the limitations of energy supply from batteries until recharged. The co-optimisation of offers in the FCAS raise and energy markets resulted in higher-priced energy offers to meet required FCAS levels and energy demand.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Constrained Heywood interconnector and decreased wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 1 August 2023. Its affected region is: Multiple NEM regions. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes is: Constrained interconnector, decreased wind generation, evening demand peak, limitations in battery energy supply.

title: Mainland volatility on Tuesday evening 1st August 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland volatility on Tuesday evening 1st August 2023" posted at 01-08-2023 06:09:00 PM is: The article discusses significant price volatility in all mainland regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on the evening of August 1, 2023, attributed to low wind production and constrained imports from Victoria due to a transmission outage. Prices soared above $10,000/MWh as the evening generation mix tightened, with solar output down for the night and wind production under 1,000MW. Notably, South Australia's price reached the Market Price Cap of $16,600/MWh for the first time at 18:35. Additionally, the NEM-wide Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) fell below 20% during certain dispatch intervals. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the evening of August 1, 2023. Its root_cause is: Low wind production and transmission outage limiting power imports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 01-08-2023. Its affected region is: All mainland regions of the NEM, with specific highlights in South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NEM, particularly in South Australia. Its cause type is: Operational inefficiencies. Its causes are: Low wind production, constrained power imports from Victoria due to transmission outage.

title: Enablement of ‘MaxAvail’ in the Bid for Semi-Scheduled units to go live (Mon 7th or Tue 8th Aug 2023) … and other enhancements in future
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Enablement of ‘MaxAvail’ in the Bid for Semi-Scheduled units to go live (Mon 7th or Tue 8th Aug 2023) … and other enhancements in future" posted at 01-08-2023 05:38:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has announced that the change to the National Electricity Market Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) supporting MaxAvail in bids for Semi-Scheduled generators is set to go live on August 7th, 2023, following a previous delay due to a memory leak in the system's updated LP Solver. This change will occur over a two-day period to ensure a smooth transition. In addition to this, AEMO plans to upgrade several key applications and processes, including Dispatch, Pre-dispatch, and PASA, thereby enhancing the management of Semi-Scheduled units and improving energy market operations. Future enhancements, such as the introduction of Fast Frequency Response and improved visibility of Medium Term Project Assessment of System Adequacy (MT PASA) data, are also in the pipeline. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, with changes beginning on August 7th and 8th, 2023. Its root_cause is: System upgrades and enhancements. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: August 7th and 8th, 2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled generators and market participants. Its cause type is: Planned system update. Its causes are: Memory leak in LP Solver, previous delays, planned enhancements to NEMDE, system upgrades.

title: Price volatility in SA on August 1, 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in SA on August 1, 2023," posted at 01-08-2023 02:53:00 PM is: On August 1, 2023, energy spot prices in South Australia (SA) experienced substantial volatility, reaching $999/MWh at multiple intervals in the early afternoon. This price spike was surprising given the usual midday abundance of cheap renewable energy in the region. The situation was compounded by constrained interconnector flows, particularly the limitation on the Heywood interconnector due to a planned outage, and significantly reduced wind power generation caused by a high-pressure system. Limited solar generation also contributed, prompting reliance on gas-powered generation to meet the market demand. These factors maintained elevated prices above $300/MWh following the initial spike. Forecasts indicated an improvement in wind power generation later in the evening, suggesting some price stabilization might occur overnight.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability and fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate effects felt on the day of the event). Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnector flow and reduced wind power generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-08-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural (weather conditions) and operational (interconnector constraint). Its causes are: Constrained Heywood interconnector due to a planned outage, large high-pressure system reducing wind power generation, and limited solar power generation.

title: Mid-July update on repairs on Callide C3 and C4 cooling tower
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mid-July update on repairs on Callide C3 and C4 cooling tower" posted at 31-07-2023 11:52:00 AM is: CS Energy provided an update on the demolition and reconstruction efforts for the Callide C3 and C4 cooling towers in central Queensland. The demolition of the C3 cooling tower is complete, and work on C4 has begun, with C3 aimed to be back in service by January 2024. No changes in service expectations have been noted since May 2023, and there hasn't been an update on the engineering report regarding the cause of the C4 failure.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until January 2024. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-05-2021, 31-07-2023. Its affected region is: Central Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes are: Unknown.

title: Engineering the transition and control of electricity
author: Kate Summers
classified_content: The summary of the news "Engineering the transition and control of electricity" posted at 21-07-2023 11:54:00 AM is: The speech by Kate Summers at the Clean Energy Summit underscores the challenges and complexities of transitioning to a high-renewable energy future. She emphasizes the need for a balanced approach that prioritizes engineering solutions over market-driven decisions to maintain system stability and prevent electrical infrastructure failures. Summers advocates for proactive power and control engineering to address potential risks in electricity supply and the importance of local storage and regulatory environments that respect engineering judgment to ensure a stable and efficient energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Transition to renewable energy and associated engineering challenges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-07-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and engineering approach. Its causes are: Challenges in transitioning to renewable energy, engineering decisions impacting electricity control, regulatory complications, and system stability concerns.

title: Let’s talk about Inertia
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Let’s talk about Inertia" posted at 20-07-2023 12:06:00 PM is: The article explores the importance of understanding inertia in the energy market, highlighting its dynamic nature and the role it plays in stabilizing the power system. The discussion is centered around the findings from the GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023, with specific case studies showing how changes in inertia are observed and analyzed in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly in regions like South Australia and Victoria. The focus is on the dependency on synchronous generators for inertia and the challenges posed by the transition to renewable energy sources without comparable inertia provision. It emphasizes the necessity for future energy systems to integrate battery energy storage systems (BESS) and renewables to maintain grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing, as real-time changes in inertia affect grid stability and pricing. Its root cause is: Transition of energy systems from traditional coal and gas-fired generation to renewable sources. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Covered incident on 4th March 2023, same date applies to the main event. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria within the NEM. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Systemic transition in energy generation. Its causes are: Decommitment of coal and gas generation, increased reliance on renewables without equivalent inertia contribution.

title: After the storm: Looking at Q2 2023 price trends, one year on from an Energy Crisis
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "After the storm: Looking at Q2 2023 price trends, one year on from an Energy Crisis" posted at 14-07-2023 03:44:00 PM is: The article examines electricity price trends in Q2 2023 across various regions within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, comparing them to past data to identify long-term trends. Notable observations include a decrease in average prices compared to Q2 2022's extreme highs, yet a continuation of the trend of increasing average prices since 2021 in regions such as Queensland, New South Wales, and South Australia. Distinct price distribution patterns, influenced by factors like regional constraints, persist across different states, leading to a widening gap between high and low price periods. Tasmania's prices remain stable, while Western Australia, due to its unique market structure, showed a more moderate price increase. The broader trend includes a notable rise in both high price incidences (>$300/MWh) and negative price incidences across all regions, indicating volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2023 and broader long-term trends. Its root_cause is: Analysis of Q2 2023 electricity price trends. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-07-2023. Its affected region is: Various regions in the NEM, including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania; Western Australia in the WEM. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market analysis and trends. Its causes is: Examination of long-term electricity price trends and regional distribution patterns influenced by past and current market dynamics.

title: Operations of coal-fired generators (Vales Point, and Eraring?) extended in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Operations of coal-fired generators (Vales Point, and Eraring?) extended in NSW" posted at 14-07-2023 02:50:00 PM is: The news reports the extension of operations for some coal units in New South Wales (NSW). Specifically, Vales Point Power Station will now operate until 2033, delayed from the original 2029 closure date. The decision is driven by uncertainties in the capacity of new generation projects and the necessity to maintain system security as renewable energy resources are integrated. Additionally, there are indications that the Eraring plant may also have its closure date extended beyond 2025 as expectations rise that it will need to continue operating longer; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to long term, as extensions can influence market stability. Its root cause is: Delay in renewable energy infrastructure and need for stable power supply. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 14-07-2023 (main date) and referenced dates include 16-03-2023 & 27-06-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Power consumers in NSW relying on stable electricity. Its cause type is: Policy and operational decisions. Its causes are: Extension of operational timelines for Vales Point and possible for Eraring due to insufficient dispatchable capacity, delays in new generation projects, and earlier closures of other coal-fired generators.

title: Peak production from Wind Farms across the NEM blasts past prior all-time record Friday evening 7th July 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Peak production from Wind Farms across the NEM blasts past prior all-time record Friday evening 7th July 2023" posted at 08-07-2023 10:39:00 AM is: The article reports that a new peak in wind farm production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) was recorded on Friday evening, 7th July 2023, reaching a level of 8,132MW. This surpassed the previous record set in June 2023 by 715MW. The article also discusses ongoing studies into the curtailment of wind and solar energy within the NEM and anticipated data analysis for Q2 2023.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: is: Long term. Root_cause is: Increased wind production capacity. Accident scale: is: Unknown. Dates: are: 08-07-2023 10:39:00 AM, 07-07-2023. Affected region: is: National Electricity Market (NEM) region. Affected key users: are: Grid operators and energy producers within the NEM. Cause type: is: Natural cause. Causes: are: Record-breaking wind production surpassing previous records, ongoing assessment of energy curtailment trends.

title: Will we see new ‘All-Time-Max’ wind across the NEM on Saturday morning 8th July 2023?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Will we see new ‘All-Time-Max’ wind across the NEM on Saturday morning 8th July 2023?" posted at 06-07-2023 02:53:00 PM is: The article discusses the forecasted potential for a new all-time maximum wind generation record across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on the morning of 8th July 2023, with wind capability predicted to reach 7,471MW. However, factors such as wind availability and possible curtailment, due to low market demand during the forecast period, could impact the realization of this new record. Readers are reminded that these forecasts are subject to change. The article also highlights that wind generation may be curtailed due to price-based reasons given low demand levels. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term/potentially immediate impact on electricity generation, with possible curtailment effects observed over the weekend. Its root_cause is: Wind generation forecasts and curtailment potential due to low demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25th June 2023, 3rd-4th July 2023, and 8th July 2023 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), which includes eastern and southern Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted natural variability and operational factors within wind generation capabilities. Its causes are: Forecasted wind capabilities with possible discrepancies due to weather changes, operational curtailment considerations, and demand fluctuations within the electricity market.

title: Mid-afternoon prices elevated with modest wind and modest solar
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mid-afternoon prices elevated with modest wind and modest solar" posted at 03-07-2023 03:20:00 PM is: The article discusses elevated electricity spot prices across all four regions of the mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) on the afternoon of July 3, 2023, with the highest prices observed in South Australia. This price increase coincides with modest outputs from both wind and solar power sources. Wind energy output was below 1,000 MW, equating to an instantaneous capacity factor of under 10% of the installed capacity at the time, while solar energy production was reduced by 33% to 50% due to widespread cloud cover. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Low renewable energy output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-07-2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, with a focus on South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Low wind output, poor solar generation due to cloud cover.

title: From the outside looking in: Checking back in with the NEM via Australian Energy Week 2023 coverage
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "From the outside looking in: Checking back in with the NEM via Australian Energy Week 2023 coverage" posted at 30-06-2023 04:19:00 PM is: The article revisits insights from the Australian Energy Week 2023, emphasizing the ongoing energy transition challenges in Australia. Key points from the conference included speeches from AEMO CEO Daniel Westerman about tensions in the energy transition and AEMC Chair Anna Collyer's focus on balancing emissions reduction with other National Energy Objectives. Media coverage highlighted concerns about the slow pace of the transition and the need for more investment. The conference coincided with tight supply-demand balances last week, which will be analyzed in the upcoming GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q2 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The slow pace of transition and need for more investment noted in the event. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-06-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Energy transition challenges. Its causes are: Slow pace of energy transition, lack of investment, need to balance emission reduction with energy objectives.

title: NEM-wide wind production record broken twice last weekend. New record as of Sunday 25th of June 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide wind production record broken twice last weekend. New record as of Sunday 25th of June 2023" posted at 28-06-2023 03:17:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced record-breaking wind production over the weekend of June 24-25, 2023, with wind output reaching 7,353MW on June 24, and then surpassing it at 7,417MW on June 25. This high level of wind production accounted for 30.73% of the NEM-wide grid demand. These records highlight the evolving trends in wind energy's contribution to the market, although further analysis is needed to assess the impact of non-dispatched wind generators. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term increase in wind energy supply. Its root cause is: High wind conditions across the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: June 24, 2023, to June 25, 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers reliant on wind power. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Particularly windy conditions leading to increased wind generation capacity.

title: A run of high prices seen in SA this morning Wednesday 28th June 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A run of high prices seen in SA this morning Wednesday 28th June 2023" posted at 28-06-2023 11:28:00 AM is: High electricity prices were observed in South Australia on the morning of June 28th, 2023, peaking at $12,922.49/MWh at 07:55 (NEM time). Despite a forecasted Lack of Reserve (LOR2) condition from 7:00 am to 8:30 am, which did not materialize, price spikes occurred due to low wind generation and reduced interconnector flow between VIC and SA. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Low wind generation and limited interconnector flow. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-06-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Supply constraints. Its causes are: Low wind output, interconnector flow limitation.

title: LOR3 forecast flickers on and off for South Australia for tomorrow evening 27th of June 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "LOR3 forecast flickers on and off for South Australia for tomorrow evening 27th of June 2023" posted at 26-06-2023 03:44:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued and later downgraded a Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) forecast for South Australia for the evening of 27th June 2023, indicating a tight supply-demand balance. The situation evolved with the latest forecasts showing a LOR2 notice for the same evening and another forecast for LOR2 on Thursday morning, indicating potential tight electricity supply conditions later in the week.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, impacting 27th June 2023 evening and potentially affecting conditions later in the week. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2023 (for the forecast and downgrade notice), and 27-06-2023 (for the impact). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System/ Grid issue. Its causes are: Oscillations in LOR forecasts, evolving supply-demand conditions in South Australia.

title: Increasing curtailment of Wind and Solar across the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increasing curtailment of Wind and Solar across the NEM" posted at 24-06-2023 11:01:00 AM is: The article discusses the increasing occurrence of curtailment of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) sources, specifically wind and solar, across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. On the 24th of June 2023, wind and large solar production approached 10,000 MW, contributing to a supply of 18,584 MW across the NEM, which led to subdued prices in regions like QLD, VIC, and SA. Economically-induced curtailment is affecting prices negatively in these areas. The article also explores the historical trends of curtailment, noting a significant increase in both absolute and relative terms from Q1 2022 to Q1 2023. The key reasons for curtailment are market dynamics and optimization strategies by VRE operators, which complicate estimation efforts. The piece anticipates challenges for project business cases and market operations due to this trend. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2023. Its root_cause is: Economically-induced curtailment of wind and solar energy due to subdued demand and other market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-06-2023. Its affected region is: NEM regions of QLD, VIC, and SA. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic and market-driven. Its causes are: Increasing production of wind and solar energy, economically induced curtailment, market optimization by operators.

title: AEMO (oscillating in) forecasting LOR2 in South Australia on Tuesday 27th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO (oscillating in) forecasting LOR2 in South Australia on Tuesday 27th June 2023" posted at 23-06-2023 06:15:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially declared a Forecast Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for South Australia for the period from 18:30 to 19:00 on 27th June 2023, due to a slight shortfall in the capacity reserve requirement. However, this forecast was briefly canceled but then reinstated later the same day. This oscillation in reserve forecasts underscores the increasing risk and supply-demand tightness within the National Electricity Market (NEM).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically the evening of 27th June 2023. Its root_cause is: Supply-demand uncertainty leading to reserve shortfall prediction. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23rd June 2023 (the notice date) and 27th June 2023 (the forecast date for the event). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted electricity reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Oscillations in supply-demand forecasts leading to tight reserve margins in South Australia.

title: Prices up in the mid-afternoon on Thursday 22nd June 2023 (a rare instance –  with solar generation lower)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prices up in the mid-afternoon on Thursday 22nd June 2023 (a rare instance –  with solar generation lower)" posted at 22-06-2023 02:37:00 PM is: The article discusses an uncommon occurrence of increased electricity prices in the afternoon of June 22, 2023, attributed to reduced solar generation, which is 1,500 MW lower than usual. This drop in generation is partly due to cloud cover affecting solar farms in the southern NEM region, although the northern solar farms in Queensland remain unaffected.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Decreased solar generation due to cloud cover. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2023. Its affected region is: Southern NEM region. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM region. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Reduced solar generation, cloud cover affecting solar farms.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Wed 21st June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Wed 21st June 2023" posted at 21-06-2023 05:49:00 PM is: Spot prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) experienced significant volatility on the evening of 21st June 2023, driven by a combination of cold winter weather increasing demand above 30,000MW and constrained exports on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Wind energy contributed reasonably, helping to maintain a higher Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) compared to the previous day. The constraints led to elevated pricing actions in QLD and NSW, with some impacts in VIC and SA, and a brief period where the NEM-wide IRPM dropped below 15%. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Cold weather increasing demand and constraints on interconnectors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-06-2023; 20-06-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System and grid constraints. Its causes are: Increased winter demand, constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector due to network outage, insufficient solar output, and wind power contribution.

title: A cold evening drives NEM-wide demand high, and IRPM low, on Tuesday 20th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A cold evening drives NEM-wide demand high, and IRPM low, on Tuesday 20th June 2023" posted at 20-06-2023 06:14:00 PM is: On the evening of June 20, 2023, NEM-wide market demand surged to 32,238MW due to cold weather, particularly affecting TAS, NSW, and QLD, with available generation at 36,024MW and an Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) at a low 11.74%. This low IRPM and elevated demand, exacerbated by low variable renewable energy (VRE) yields, led to spot prices reaching $1,000/MWh across mainland regions, highlighting the need for more firming capacity amid the energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing. Its root_cause is: Elevated electricity demand and low VRE yield. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2023. Its affected region is: TAS, NSW, QLD. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in mainland regions. Its cause type is: Weather and generation-related. Its causes are: Elevated demand due to cold weather, low solar and wind generation, insufficient dispatchable capacity.

title: What happened to the  ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint equation leading to whiplash on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on Sunday 18th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What happened to the ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint equation leading to whiplash on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on Sunday 18th June 2023" posted at 19-06-2023 04:25:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant shift in electricity flow on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on June 18th, 2023, due to the 'N::N_DTKV_2' constraint equation involved in the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley line outage. This constraint limited exports from Victoria to New South Wales, causing a rapid change when the TUMUT3 hydro unit became a cheaper energy source, leading to a market price floor scenario; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate impact during the dispatch interval on June 18th, 2023). Its root_cause is: Outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2023. Its affected region is: VIC1-NSW1 interconnector area. Its affected key users are: Typically electricity market participants and operators. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Constraint equation 'N::N_DTKV_2', line outage on Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV, market reaction to price changes.

title: Industrial Action at Longford
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Industrial Action at Longford" posted at 19-06-2023 12:01:00 PM is: The article discusses the resolution of a Protected Industrial Action (PIA) involving Esso Australia's Longford gas production, as announced by AEMO. Although the PIA initially affected operations, the gas production levels have returned to normal following discussions at the Fair Work Commission. The vessels Skandi Darwin and Skandi Feistein, which support gas production from Bass Strait operations, have been exempted from the PIA. However, the Skandi Darwin's work for maintenance and decommissioning remains subject to the PIA but has no expected impact on gas production.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as gas production has returned to planned levels. Its root_cause is: Protected Industrial Action affecting gas production operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-06-2023. Its affected region is: Longford, Bass Strait operations. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Industrial action. Its causes are: Industrial action affecting support operations for gas production vessels.

title: Whiplash on VIC1-NSW1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Whiplash on VIC1-NSW1" posted at 18-06-2023 07:40:00 PM is: The article describes significant changes in electricity flow across the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, where a reversal of 1,404MW in target flow occurred between the 17:00 and 17:05 dispatch intervals on 18th June 2023. At 17:00, 1,043MW was exported from Victoria to New South Wales, which reversed to 361MW being imported from New South Wales to Victoria by 17:05. This change was influenced by the ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint due to an ongoing outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV line. Simultaneously, NSW generation increased by 1,464MW, while Victoria's generation decreased by 818MW amid rising market demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Line constraint due to an outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2023. Its affected region is: VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Constraint-induced flow reversal. Its causes are: Reversal in target flow due to the 'N::N_DTKV_2' constraint, outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley line.

title: Transmission outage of the Dapto -to- Kangaroo Valley transmission link in southern NSW (line 18) … and spot volatility
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transmission outage of the Dapto -to- Kangaroo Valley transmission link in southern NSW (line 18) … and spot volatility" posted at 14-06-2023 01:27:00 PM is: The article discusses the transmission outage on the Dapto-Kangaroo Valley transmission link (‘line 18’) in southern NSW, which occurred from May 15 to June 3, 2023, and its impact on electricity spot price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). This outage was identified as a contributor to the spot price fluctuations due to its limitation on secure northward power flows, inducing volatility during peak demand periods. The outage is part of a broader set of factors, including other transmission constraints and plant retirements like Liddell, impacting the electricity market. Constraint sets, including ‘N-DTKV_18_WG_CLOSE’ and ‘N-DTKV_18_WG_OPEN’, were invoked during this period, demonstrating the complexity of the National Electricity Market's operations. The outage resumed in June and is expected to cause potential volatility through the end of that month. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid-May to the end of June 2023. Its root_cause is: Transmission line outage. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 15-05-2023 to 03-06-2023 (initial outage) and resumed 13-06-2023 to 19-06-2023 and 30-06-2023. Its affected region is: Southern New South Wales, impacting interconnections with Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Infrastructure maintenance/outage. Its causes are: Prolonged transmission line outage, reduced flow capabilities, constraints from line limits, plant retirements, and generation limitations due to seasonal factors.

title: Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 12th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 12th June 2023" posted at 12-06-2023 07:09:00 PM is: Volatility in electricity spot prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) was observed on June 12, 2023, due to multiple factors including the outage of Kogan Creek and Millmerran 2 generators. This led to prices exceeding $1,000/MWh starting from the 17:10 dispatch interval. A key issue was the 'N>>NIL_33_34' transmission constraint, affecting interconnectors between VIC, NSW, and QLD, thereby preventing lower VIC prices from influencing NSW and QLD. Additionally, the decline in solar and wind energy contributions around sunset led to increased demand for alternative energy dispatch. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, on June 12, 2023. title: Four coal unit outages in the past 24 hours
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four coal unit outages in the past 24 hours" posted at 12-06-2023 02:55:00 PM is: The article reports on the unexpected outages of four coal units within the past 24 hours in the National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically Kogan Creek, Loy Yang A2, and Yallourn units 3 and 4. These outages were not planned in advance, as evidenced by the lack of pre-determined outage forecasts. The outages contribute to the ongoing challenges faced by the NEM in managing the energy supply following the closure of the Liddell coal plant. Observations from the GenInsights Quarterly Update series indicate that while there is a significant portion of coal unit unavailability, there hasn't been an increase in the frequency of these trippings over time. Additionally, differences in planned maintenance schedules are highlighted between the units affected. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting current operations and potentially influencing short-term electricity supply reliability. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of coal units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-06-2023. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) region in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Unplanned outages of Kogan Creek, Loy Yang A2, Yallourn units 3 and 4.

title: Record for NEM-wide wind production nudged slightly higher on Thu 8th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Record for NEM-wide wind production nudged slightly higher on Thu 8th June 2023" posted at 09-06-2023 11:57:00 AM is: The article reports a new record for NEM-wide wind production on June 8, 2023, reaching 7,324 MW, surpassing the previous record set in August 2022. This wind production level had a significant share of over 30% of the grid demand. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared a "Protected Event" in South Australia due to destructive wind conditions on June 7, but this was later canceled. Despite operational challenges, such as potential transmission element losses, wind production is expected to increase further due to added capacity. The report emphasizes the potential for increased wind generation records in the coming months and encourages readers to follow trends using updated software tools. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Less Relevant. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High wind production reaching unprecedented levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 8th June 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide with specific constraints in South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational and environmental factors. Its causes are: High wind speeds, unmet generation forecasts, and additional wind capacity.

AEMO's reference to a previous media release from 16th March 2023 reinforces the ongoing concerns about gas supply. title: Digging further into ‘Aggregate Scheduled Target’, to understand what Dispatchable Capacity is actually required
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Digging further into ‘Aggregate Scheduled Target’, to understand what Dispatchable Capacity is actually required" posted at 31-05-2023 06:00:00 PM is: The article delves into the necessity of establishing sufficient dispatchable capacity to replace retiring coal plants, emphasizing the challenges of incentivizing new and existing dispatchable plants to participate in the market. It outlines trends in the Aggregate Scheduled Target (AggSchedTarget) from 2009 onward, revealing a decreasing demand for scheduled plants and increased unpredictability due to weather dependencies. The piece highlights different seasonal requirements, noting more variability in summer necessitating flexible commercial support, while winter demands are consistently predictable around peak evening hours when solar output fades. This comprehensive analysis stresses the importance of understanding the full opportunity for dispatchable capacity within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since the analysis covers years from 2009 to Q1 2023 and looks to the future. Its root_cause is: Transition from coal to renewable energy sources causing a shift in dispatchable capacity needs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) wide. Its affected key users are: Power companies and electricity market regulators. Its cause type is: Structural change in energy supply systems. Its causes are: Shortage of firming capacity, high dependency on weather conditions, retirement of coal plants, and rising renewable energy integration without adequate replacement of dispatchable capacity.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 released" posted at 31-05-2023 02:49:00 PM is: Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting have collaboratively released the GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 on 31st May 2023. This fourth report of its series, now 258 pages long, continues to explore various aspects of the energy transition in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). The update includes an Executive Summary with key observations and six detailed appendices that cover metrics like system inertia, grid demand changes, performance of wind and solar assets, dispatchable capacity analysis, coal unit operations, and network dynamics. These analyses are aimed at understanding the evolving challenges faced by the NEM in terms of energy production, reliability, and transition towards renewable resources. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to Long term. Its root_cause is: N/A. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity Market Analysts, Policymakers, Energy Consultants. Its cause type is: Analytic/Report Release. Its causes are: Detailed performance reports and analysis on NEM, including system inertia, grid demand, renewable energy integration, and coal unit dependability.

Market volatility was observed on several dates in May, though similar issues were noted in South Australia; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather.
